El mundo material no es falso. Porque proviene de la verdad es real, pero las manifestaciones de este mundo son temporales. Es la creencia de que el universo material es permanente lo que es falso.
lessons.content_en:
PREDICATION

Today, many scientists are propagating the doctrine that life originates from matter. However, they cannot provide evidence, either experimentally or theoretically. In fact, they maintain their position essentially based on faith, in the face of all kinds of scientific objections. Srila Prabhupada points out that this unfounded dogma has caused great harm to moral and spiritual standards worldwide and has thus resulted in immeasurable suffering.

One of the great challenges for the Krsna consciousness movement is to expose this misconception and establish its viewpoint as the most viable and comprehensive explanation of reality.

Atheistic Science

Since the time of Newton, science has maintained that phenomena can be described (at least in principle) in terms of measurable quantities that can be calculated using simple mathematical laws. This premise, which we call the principle of reductionism, implies that reality is essentially simple and that human beings, solely through the power of their minds and senses, can ultimately fully understand the nature and origin of all phenomena in the universe. Although the principle of reductionism is very basic, it has provided the underlying strategy for scientific research, and as scientists have moved from one success to another, their faith in the universal applicability of this principle has grown ever stronger.

However, the unconditional acceptance of the principle of reductionism has some extremely disturbing consequences. It reduces the universe to a mechanism that operates according to impersonal laws, and it reduces the individual human being to a complex whose "will" and "feelings" correspond only to patterns of chemical interactions between molecules.

As a result, values and ethical standards can no longer be understood as fundamental principles that originate from a transcendental creator who defines the ultimate purpose and meaning of life. Instead, they become mere survival strategies that arose by chance, perpetuated due to their effectiveness under certain circumstances, and will be disregarded by an inexorable physical transformation as those circumstances change. In this regard, physicist Wolfgang Pauli predicted: "We may reach a point in the not-so-distant future where the parables and images of ancient religions will have lost their persuasive power even for the average person; when that happens, I fear that all the old ethics will collapse like a house of cards and unimaginable horrors will be perpetrated."

Given the serious implications of the reductionist approach of modern science, we should hesitate to accept it as completely valid unless truly compelling evidence forces us to do so.

Many scientists and philosophers argue that such evidence has already been found in abundance. However, a detailed examination of current scientific theories reveals that this is simply not the case. Although scientists have undoubtedly made many significant discoveries, they have rushed to claim that they have demonstrated that the universal system is based on the principle of reductionism.

Krsna, Christos, Christ

Year 1974. Near the ISKCON center in Frankfurt am Main, Germany, Srila Prabhupada and several of his disciples go for a morning walk with Father Emmanuel Jungclaussen, a Benedictine monk from the Niederalteich monastery. Father Emmanuel, noticing that Srila Prabhupada is carrying meditation beads similar to a rosary, explains that he also continuously prays a prayer: "Lord Jesus Christ, have mercy on us." The following conversation resulted from this:

Srila Prabhupada: What is the meaning of the word Christ?

Father Emmanuel: Christ comes from the Greek "christos," which means "the anointed one."

Srila Prabhupada: "Christos" is the Greek version of the word Krsna.

Father Emmanuel: That is very interesting.

Srila Prabhupada: When a Hindu invokes Krsna, he often says "Krsta." "Krsta" is a Sanskrit word that means "attraction." So, when we address God as "Christ," "Krsta," or "Krsna," we are referring to the same fully attractive Supreme Personality of God. When Jesus said, "Our Father, who art in heaven, hallowed be Thy name," that name of God was "Krsta" or "Krsna." Do you agree?

Father Emmanuel: I believe that Jesus, as the Son of God, has revealed to us the true name of God: Christ. We can call God "Father," but if we want to address Him by His true name, we must say "Christ."

Srila Prabhupada: Yes. "Christ" is another way of saying "Krsta," and "Krsta" is another way of pronouncing "Krsna," the name of God. Jesus said that one should glorify the name of God, but yesterday I heard a theologian say that God has no name, that we can only call Him "Father." A son may call his father "Father," but the father also has a specific name. Similarly, "God" is the general name of the Supreme Personality of God, whose specific name is "Krsna." Therefore, whether you call God "Christ," "Krsta," or "Krsna," in the end, you are addressing the same Supreme Personality of God.

Father Emmanuel: Yes. If we speak of the true name of God, then we must say "Christos." In our religion, we have the Trinity: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. We believe that we can know the name of God only through the revelation of the Son of God. Jesus Christ revealed the name of the Father, and therefore we take the name "Christ" as the revealed name of God.

Srila Prabhupada: Actually, it doesn't matter—"Krsna" or "Christ"—the name is the same. The main thing is to follow the mandates of the Vedic scriptures that recommend chanting the name of God in this age. The easiest way is to chant the maha-mantra: Hare Krsna, Hare Krsna, Krsna Krsna, Hare Hare / Hare Rama, Hare Rama, Rama Rama, Hare Hare. "Rama" and "Krsna" are names of God, and "Hare" is the energy of God. So, when we chant the maha-mantra, we are addressing God, and also His energy. This energy is of two kinds: spiritual and material. Currently, we find ourselves in the clutches of the material energy. Therefore, we pray to Krsna asking Him to kindly liberate us from serving the material energy and accept that we serve the spiritual energy. This is the essence of our philosophy. "Hare Krsna" means: "Oh, energy of God! Oh, God (Krsna)! Please engage me in Your service." Our nature is to serve. In one way or another, we have put ourselves in the service of material things, but when this service transforms into service to the spiritual energy, then our life is perfect. Practicing bhakti-yoga (loving service to God) means being free from designations such as "Hindu," "Muslim," "Christian," this or that, and simply serving God. We have created the Christian, Hindu, and Muslim religions, but when we come to a designation-free religion, in which we do not think of ourselves as Hindus or Christians or Muslims, we can then speak of pure religion, of bhakti.

Father Emmanuel: Mukti?

Srila Prabhupada: No, bhakti. When we talk about bhakti, mukti (liberation from material suffering) is included. Without bhakti, there is no mukti, but if we act on the platform of bhakti, then mukti is included. We learn this from the Bhagavad-gita (14.26):

mam ca yo’vyabhicarena  
bhakti-yogena sevate  
sa gunan samatityaitan  
brahma-bhuyaya kalpate

"One who is fully engaged in devotional service, unwavering in all circumstances, transcends the modes of material nature and thus becomes eligible to be absorbed in the Brahman."

Father Emmanuel

: Is Brahman Krsna?

Srila Prabhupada

: Krsna is Parabrahman. Brahman is understood in three aspects: as impersonal Brahman, as localized Paramatma, and as personal Brahman. Krsna is personal, and He is the Supreme Brahman, for God is ultimately a person. This is confirmed in the Srimad-Bhagavatam (1.2.11):

vadanti tat tattva-vidas  
tattvam yaj jñanam advayam  
brahmeti paramatmeti  
bhagavan iti sabdyate  

"The transcendentalists who know the Absolute Truth call that non-dual substance Brahman, Paramatma, or Bhagavan." The aspect of the Supreme Personality is the highest understanding of God. He possesses the six opulences in full: He is the strongest, the richest, the most beautiful, the most famous, the wisest, and the most renounced.

Father Emmanuel

: Yes, I agree.

Srila Prabhupada

: Because God is absolute, His name, form, and qualities are also absolute and not different from Him. Therefore, chanting the holy name of God means being directly with Him. When we are in contact with God, we acquire divine qualities, and by completely purifying ourselves, we become constant companions of the Supreme Lord.

Father Emmanuel

: But our understanding of God's name is limited.

Srila Prabhupada

: Yes, we are limited, but God is unlimited. And because He is unlimited, or absolute, He has unlimited names, and each one of them is God. We can understand His names to the extent that our spiritual understanding is developed.

Father Emmanuel

: May I ask you a question? We Christians also preach love for God, and we try to experience love for God and offer service with all our heart and soul. Now, what is the difference between your movement and ours? Why do you send your disciples to Western countries to preach love for God when the Gospel of Jesus Christ presents the same message?

Srila Prabhupada

: The problem is that Christians do not follow God's commandments. Do you agree?

Father Emmanuel

: Yes, you are largely correct.

Srila Prabhupada

: Then, what is the meaning of Christians' love for God? If we do not follow God's orders, then where is our love? Therefore, we have come to teach what it means to love God: if we love Him, we cannot disobey His orders. And if we are disobedient, our love is not true.

In the whole world, people do not love God, but rather their dogs. The movement for Krsna consciousness is therefore necessary to teach people to revive their forgotten love for God. Not only Christians, but also Hindus, Muslims, and everyone else are guilty. They have stamped themselves with the titles of "Christian," "Hindu," or "Muslim," but they do not obey God. That is the problem.

Visitor

: Could you tell me in what way Christians are disobedient?

Srila Prabhupada

: Yes. The first point is that they violate the commandment "Thou shalt not kill" by maintaining slaughterhouses. Do you agree that this commandment is being violated?

Father Emmanuel

: Personally, I agree.

Srila Prabhupada

: Good. So, if Christians want to love God, they must stop killing animals.

Father Emmanuel

: But isn't the most important point...?

Srila Prabhupada

: But if one point is overlooked, there will be an error in the calculations. After that, it does not matter what is added or subtracted; there is already an error in the calculations, and everything that follows will also be wrong. We cannot simply accept the part of Scripture that we like and reject what we do not like, and still expect to achieve the result. For example, a hen lays eggs with its rear end and eats with its beak. The farmer might think, "The front part of the hen is very expensive because I have to feed it. I better cut it off." But if the head is missing, there will be no more eggs, because the body will be dead. Similarly, if we reject the difficult parts of Scripture and obey the parts we like, that interpretation will not help us. We must accept all the mandates of Scripture as they are given, not just those that suit us. If the first order, "Thou shalt not kill," is not followed, then how can we speak of love for God?

Visitor

: Christians interpret that commandment as applying to humans, not to animals.

Srila Prabhupada

: That would mean that Christ was not intelligent enough to use the right word: "murder." There is "slaughter," and there is "murder." Murder refers to human beings. Do you think Jesus was not intelligent enough to use the right word—"murder"—instead of the word "kill"? Killing refers to any kind of slaughter, and especially the slaughter of animals. If Jesus had simply referred to the slaughter of human beings, He would have used the word "murder."

Father Emmanuel

: But in the Old Testament, the commandment "Thou shalt not kill" does refer to murder. And when Jesus said, "Thou shalt not kill," He extended that commandment to mean that a human being should not only refrain from killing another human being but should also treat them with love. He never spoke about the relationship of man with other living entities, only about his relationship with other human beings. When He said, "Thou shalt not kill," He also meant in the mental and emotional sense that one should not insult anyone or hurt them or treat them badly, etc.

Srila Prabhupada

: We are not interested in this or that testament, but only in the words used in the commandments. If you want to interpret those words, that is another matter. We understand the direct meaning. "Thou shalt not kill" means: "Christians should not kill." You may present interpretations in order to continue today's way of acting, but we understand very clearly that there is no need for interpretation. Interpretation is needed if things are not clear. But here the meaning is clear. "Thou shalt not kill" is a clear instruction. Why should we interpret it?

Father Emmanuel

: Is not killing also the eating of plants?

Srila Prabhupada

: The Vaisnava philosophy teaches us that we should not even unnecessarily kill plants. In the Bhagavad-gita (9.26), Krsna says:

patram puspam phalam toyam  
yo me bhaktya prayacchati  
tad aham bhakty-upahrtam  
asnami prayatatmanah  

"If someone offers Me with love and devotion a leaf, a flower, a fruit, or water, I will accept it." We offer Krsna only the kind of food He requests, and then we eat the remnants. If offering vegetarian food to Krsna were sinful, then it would be Krsna's sin, not ours. But God is apapa-viddha: sinful reactions do not apply to Him. He is like the sun, which is so powerful that it can purify even urine, something impossible for us to do. Krsna is also like a king, who can order that a murderer be hanged, but he himself is exempt from punishment for being very powerful. Eating food that was first offered to the Lord is also like the case of a soldier who kills during war. In a war, when the commander orders a man to attack, the obedient soldier who kills the enemy will receive a medal. But if the same soldier kills someone on his own, he will be punished. Similarly, when we eat only prasadam (the remnants of food offered to Krsna), we do not commit any sin. This is confirmed in the Bhagavad-gita (3.13):

yajña-sistasinah santo  
mucyante sarva-kilbisaih  
bhuñjate te tv agham papa  
ye pacanty atma-karanat

**Bhakti Yoga Course Content**

"Devotees of the Lord are freed from all kinds of sins because they take food that has been offered beforehand as a sacrifice. Others, those who prepare food for the enjoyment of their own senses, truly eat only sin."

**Father Emmanuel**: Can’t Krsna give permission to eat animals?

**Srila Prabhupada**: Yes, in the animal kingdom. But a civilized human being, a religious human being, should not kill and eat animals. If you stop killing animals and chant the holy name of Christ, everything will be perfect. I have not come to teach you, but only to ask you to please chant the name of God. The Bible also demands this of you. So, let us cooperate and chant, and if you have any prejudice against chanting the name Krsna, then chant "Cristos" or "Krsta"; there is no difference. Sri Caitanya said: namnam akari bahudha nija-sarva-saktih: "God has many millions of names, and since there is no difference between the name of God and He Himself, each of these names has the same potency as God." Therefore, even if we accept designations such as "Hindu," "Christian," or "Muslim," if we simply chant the name of God found in our own Scriptures, we will reach the spiritual plane. The purpose of human life is self-realization: to learn to love God. That is the true beauty of man. Whether you carry out that duty as a Hindu, Christian, or Muslim does not matter, but do it!

**Father Emmanuel**: I agree.

**Srila Prabhupada**: (pointing to a meditation rosary of one hundred eight beads): We always have this rosary, just as you have yours. You are praying, but why don’t other Christians pray too? Why would they overlook this opportunity they have as human beings? Dogs and cats cannot pray, but we can because we have a human tongue. If we pray the holy names of God, we cannot lose anything; on the contrary, we gain immensely.

My disciples constantly chant Hare Krsna. They could also go to the movies or do so many other things, but they have given everything up. They do not eat fish, meat, or eggs; they do not take drugs, drink, smoke, gamble, speculate, or engage in illicit sexual relations. But they do chant the holy name of God. If you wish to cooperate with us, then go to the churches and pray "Cristo," "Krsta," or "Krsna." What objection could there be?

**Father Emmanuel**: There is none. For my part, I would love to join you.

**Srila Prabhupada**: No, we are speaking to you as a representative of the Christian church. Instead of keeping the churches closed, why not give them to us? We would chant the holy name of God there twenty-four hours a day. In many places, we have bought churches that were practically closed because no one visited them. In London, I saw hundreds of churches that were closed or being used for mundane purposes. We bought one of those churches in Los Angeles. They sold it to us because no one went there, but if you visit that same church today, you will see thousands of people. Any intelligent person can understand what God is in five minutes; it does not require five hours.

**Father Emmanuel**: I understand now.

**Srila Prabhupada**: But people do not. Their disease is that they do not want to understand.

**Visitor**: I believe that understanding God is not a matter of intelligence, but a matter of humility.

**Srila Prabhupada**: Humility means intelligence. The humble and meek possess the kingdom of God. That is stated in the Bible, is it not? But the philosophy of the shameless is that everyone is God, and today that idea has become popular. So, no one is humble and meek. If everyone thinks they are God, why should they be humble and meek? Therefore, I teach my disciples how to become humble and meek. They always offer respectful obeisances in the temple and to the spiritual master, and in this way, they advance. The qualities of humility and meekness lead very quickly to spiritual understanding. In the Vedic Scriptures, it is said: "To those who have firm faith in God and in the spiritual master, who is His representative, the meaning of the Vedic Scriptures is revealed."

**Father Emmanuel**: But shouldn’t this humility be offered to everyone else as well?

**Srila Prabhupada**: Yes, but there are two kinds of respect: special and common. Sri Krsna Caitanya taught that we should not expect honor for ourselves, but we should always respect everyone else, even if they are disrespectful to us. But to God and His pure devotee, special respect should be offered.

**Father Emmanuel**: Yes, I agree.

**Srila Prabhupada**: I believe that Christian priests should cooperate with the Krsna consciousness movement. They should chant the name of Cristo or Cristos, and they should not allow the killing of animals to continue. This program follows the teachings of the Bible; I did not invent this philosophy. Please act accordingly, and you will see how the situation in the world will change.

**Father Emmanuel**: I am very grateful to you.

**Srila Prabhupada**: Hare Krsna.

---

**Commonly Asked Questions to Devotees**

**Why do you shave your head?**

It is our trademark. It identifies us as vaisnavas (devotees of Lord Krsna). Just as doctors or police officers have their uniforms so they can be identified, this is the dress of a devotee of Krsna so that people can easily identify us and ask questions about spiritual life. Shaving the head is not absolutely necessary for spiritual advancement, but it helps to free oneself from vanity and false pride. It is a sign of renunciation and is very clean.

**Why don’t women shave their heads?**

Men and women are different in many ways, and how they practice spiritual life is also different. Traditionally, men shave their heads, but women do not. Not all men shave their heads either; some do and others do not.

**What is that mark on your forehead?**

These marks, called "tilaka," indicate that we are devotees of Krsna. They mark the body as a temple. Within the body reside the soul and God. One should learn to see this way. Instead of making so many material designations based on our bodily differences, we should see each other as spiritually related brothers. People suffer due to the lack of such spiritual vision. The goal of our movement is to teach people to see themselves as spiritual souls, servants of God.

**Why does your movement consider women less intelligent?**

We do not say that women are less intelligent. That men and women are different does not mean that one is automatically superior to the other. In fact, Krsna consciousness is the true liberation of women because we are all equally wonderful from a spiritual perspective.

**What do you do for fun?**

Our whole life is recreation. Our occupation is to sing, dance, and celebrate. Who else sings and dances at 4:30 in the morning?

All activities related to the Lord are a source of spiritual happiness. It is much more satisfying than material fun. In material happiness, the realization or achievement is always less than the expectation. In spiritual happiness, the realization is much greater than the expectation. Because we are experiencing a pleasure much higher than material happiness, we can easily renounce it. It is a superior taste.

Sex is a wonderful and natural part of life. Why do you deny that natural pleasure?

Our true nature is not the impulses of the body; it is the relationship of the soul with God. Moreover, everyone knows that sex is not satisfying. A wonderful pleasure is one that is always increasing; spiritual pleasure is the truly wonderful pleasure. Sex has its place in marriage, but its importance is overvalued in our society.

What do you think?

Krsna is the Supreme Personality of God, and we are His eternal servants. Our real place is not here in this material world, but in the spiritual world with Krsna. We are here because we have the foolish desire to enjoy things separate from God, rather than in our proper relationship with Him. Krsna consciousness is not a matter of beliefs. It can be tested and experienced. That is why it is a science.

Do you believe in Jesus Christ?

We don’t just believe in him; we follow him, which is what is important. Jesus Christ is a pure devotee of God, sent by the Lord to liberate us from material existence. Because he is giving us the path to reach God, he is as good as God, but he is not God.

What is wrong with eating meat? Christ ate meat.

First of all, it is not certain that Christ ate meat. The Bible has been translated, and in many places, it may have been altered. Furthermore, what is important is what is right, and from any conceivable point of view, the perspective of eating meat is wrong. It is cruel, it is bad for your body, it is economically disastrous. Why do it?

Why do you worship idols?

We worship Krsna, who is God. The Deities of the Lord are His authorized forms, which help us focus our minds on Him, so that we can see Him and serve Him; that is His causeless mercy upon us. An idol is an invented form to represent an imaginary idea. If you follow sports or read "People" magazine, you are the one who worships idols, not us.

Aren't you limiting God by saying He has a form?

No. You are limiting God by saying He has no form. He has an unlimited spiritual form. From His form comes all creation, both spiritual and material. He has an impersonal aspect and a personal aspect. The supreme original form of God is that of Sri Krsna.

Wouldn't your preaching be more effective if you dressed like Westerners?

Sometimes it is, and sometimes it isn't. In certain situations, preaching is better if we wear robes. However, when people are too materially attached to appreciate the dress of a monk, then we wear common Western clothing.

Aren't you escaping reality by being a Hare Krsna devotee?

Define reality. Is reality this temporary material residence or the eternal nature of the soul? We follow all the scriptures in affirming that reality is our eternal loving relationship with God. That is, of course, wonderful. Do you really oppose the fact that we have chosen to be happy instead of miserable? If you are saying that happiness is an illusion and misery is reality, that is a definition of reality that we see no reason to share.

How can you criticize modern science and technology while at the same time using modern means and machinery?

We do not condemn science and technology. We condemn the worship of them, the attitude that science and technology are the only important things in the world. They are means to an end, tools that can be useful if used properly. We believe that people would be happier if life were materially simpler, if they had fewer distractions to focus on their relationship with God, but we take the world as we find it. Utility is the principle.

Haven't you lost your individuality by becoming a devotee, since everyone dresses and looks the same?

Clothing is not important. At most, it is an indication of social position. These are the clothes of a monk; they are easy to maintain, economical, and comfortable. Of course, as devotees, we agree with our philosophy, but we use our individual talents individually in the service of Krsna. By being servants of Krsna, we are more than the people who, without thinking, follow a fashion and pursue socially imposed goals of maximizing sense gratification.

Isn't this constant chanting a form of hypnosis?

No. Hypnosis is a form of sleep, but chanting the name of God brings greater understanding and great clarity of intelligence. Don’t take my word for it; try it yourself. It works.

Why do you worship an ordinary man (the guru)?

The genuine guru is not an ordinary man. He is not worshiped as God, but as the representative of God. Since he gives us Krsna, we offer him all respects. That is simple gratitude, nothing more.

You are not receiving any salary; how do you know you are not being exploited?

Our movement is very widespread, and each temple is independent from a managerial point of view, so it is up to each person to know that their line of authority also functions without personal material motivations.

How can one distinguish a genuine guru from a false one?

The qualification of the guru is that he delivers the message of God as established in the scriptures and tradition, without alteration or adulteration. He must be of saintly character and come from a succession of spiritual masters. Perhaps most importantly, he must actively participate in the mission of his spiritual master. By following his instructions, does love of God develop? Does one feel detached from the material concept of life?

If men and women are spiritually equal, why are there no women gurus or leaders in your movement?

The qualification to be a guru is not whether one is a man or a woman, but how much one surrenders to God. There are many examples of women gurus and spiritual leaders in our tradition. (Show them a copy of the Teachings of Queen Kunti).

If your religion is so wonderful, why are there so many problems in India, where it comes from?

It does not come from India; it comes from God. There was a time when India was very religious, when people closely followed the scriptures, and India was the most prosperous country in the world. Now, they are not following the scriptures; they are trying to imitate the materialism of the West. Therefore, they are suffering. If one neglects the laws of God, then they suffer.

All religions claim to have the answer. So how is your religion different?

We do not say that we are the best. We say that the process that helps you develop love for God is the best. You have to judge by the result. Who is really developing love for God?

Where do you get the money to maintain the movement?

The Lord maintains all living entities throughout the universe, so He will surely maintain His devotees. We raise money by selling books, running restaurants, and receiving donations from married members who work, the congregation, and life members, as well as from the general public.

Why do you harass people by asking for money?

Begging implies that one is lazy to work for their needs; that is why we do not ask. We solicit donations not for ourselves, but to help suffering humanity. Many people appreciate our literature. If the few who are bothered took the time to understand how we use the funds, they would not oppose us.

Can't you do something more useful for society than chant all day?

We do not just chant all day. Devotees engage in many activities, publishing books, building farms and schools, making films, and distributing food and literature to thousands. Moreover, chanting is a process to revive our natural consciousness of God. This is actually the most important activity in human society. If more people took the time to understand the basic principles of God consciousness, the problems of human society would be resolved.

One cannot see the soul or God, so why believe in something we cannot see?

Why do you believe in atoms, viruses, or the dark side of the moon? You cannot see them. There are many things whose existence we accept without being able to perceive them directly. One can accept the existence of the soul and God by their symptoms. The life or consciousness within the body, and the perfect symmetry and details of the universe are symptoms that indicate the existence of the soul and a Supreme Being. We can accept by faith that there are spiritually realized individuals who can truly see and know God. And as one becomes more and more purified by following a genuine process of self-realization, one will also be able to see the soul and God.

Parents and Family

Human life differs from animal life in that humans can inquire about the nature of the Absolute and can choose to comply with the laws of God. Human parenthood, likewise, is not merely for the purpose of reproducing the species, but rather to allow one to free oneself from the imprisonment of this material body.

Nowadays, people generally have children as an unwanted byproduct of sexual life, and even if the child is "wanted" from conception, parents rarely have knowledge of the child's actual needs. Therefore, children tend to distrust and disrespect their parents and other authorities, believing that these individuals are not qualified to adequately fulfill their responsibilities. In Krsna consciousness, children can greatly respect their parents and other authorities due to their impeccable character.

Having clearly understood these points, a devotee is not a fanatic who rejects their parents as "maya." Rather, they are gentle and compassionate, knowing how and when to preach to elevate everyone, including their parents. After millions of births, in the material world, we have finally had the opportunity to know Krsna and engage in devotional service. Clearly, our parents had something to do with this blessed event, and therefore they must be greatly respected. They may be attached to their bodies, but if we use our bodies in the service of the Lord and thus become spiritualized, they will also benefit.

Upon reaching Krsna consciousness, we realize that we did not love anyone before, but now, by awakening our true love for Krsna, we naturally love all His parts and parcels, including our parents. Instead of loving our parents less now, we love them more, because we care for the real person, the soul, and we also have more love to give.

There is a natural bond of affection between father and son. Therefore, devotees should regularly keep in touch with their parents through letters, phone calls, and visits, and remember them on birthdays, anniversaries, Christmas, etc. We should not be afraid to talk a little with them about their everyday matters. At least initially, it is sufficient preaching that they accept the fact that we now rise early to pray, have shaved our heads, and have become vegetarians, etc. Our presence reminds them of Krsna, and they may feel embarrassed to smoke, eat meat, or do similar things in front of us. We must be patient and not really preach to them; they are connected to us, and we to them, for a long time to come.

Sometimes parents are unreasonably hostile; this creates difficulties. A devotee must be tolerant and not take illogical criticisms too seriously, just as one would not take too seriously the comments of an intoxicated person or a small child. Such parents generally calm down and begin to appreciate Krsna consciousness after about a year. Initially, we have to avoid situations that make them blaspheme and not provoke them by committing the ninth offense against the holy name (preaching to the non-receptive).

At first, the most important thing is to become firm and strong in spiritual life without distractions. It is recommended that during the first three months, one remain in the temple and follow the program of Bhaktas (or Bhaktins). Instead of visiting relatives during that period, it is better to keep in touch by letter or phone. Of course, they are welcome to visit you at the temple. But if after a couple of weeks you show up at the door with a shaved head, a skirt, and a bead bag, that is obviously a shock, and it is not easy for them to adjust. A more gradual approach is better. Let them know that you are trying this for a time to see if you want it before making final decisions or commitments. This will avoid many unnecessary worries and sorrows.

Mayavada Philosophy

The Mayavada philosophy was inaugurated by Sripad Sankaracharya to defeat Buddhism. Mayavadis believe that the highest truth is Brahman or spirit, which is unlimited and formless and inactive. They maintain that all living entities are one with Brahman but are currently covered by illusion and thus separated from Brahman. When the illusion is dispelled, the living entities return to being one with Brahman and lose their individuality.

From this perspective, Mayavadis do not accept the form and personality of Krsna as supreme, but rather consider them products of maya. Because they maintain this viewpoint, Lord Caitanya described Mayavadis as the greatest offenders against Krsna.

The Mayavadi philosophy is the most widely known form of religious thought in the West, generally under the erroneous general name of "Vedanta." The impersonal essence of Mayavada thought is also fundamental to the "philosophies" of most Westerners who are not dogmatic followers of the Bible.

Most people do not have enough information about the transcendental form of God, so they are greatly influenced by impersonalism. When speaking with those who accept Vedic authority, we can easily establish the Vaisnava position by citing scriptures. However, since most people we encounter have little or no faith in the Scriptures, we must defend our philosophy on the basis of logic and argument. There are several glaring defects in the Mayavada philosophy that are worth remembering to discuss these matters.

Since we have personality, how is it that our ultimate source lacks personality? If having personality is a good thing, as it seems to be the case, we would appear to be superior to our impersonal source. The Vedanta-sutra states that the Absolute Truth is the source of everything. Therefore, personality must be present in the Absolute Truth.

They say that the impersonal Brahman manifests in the material world in a personal form. How does personality emerge from impersonality? Where do we have any experience of such a phenomenon? In the Bhagavad-gita (7.24), Krsna declares that those who think their personality comes from something impersonal lack intelligence.

Brahman is unchangeable. How could it fragment temporarily like the individual souls of the material world? And why would it choose to do so? The Bhagavad-gita (15.7) explains that the souls are eternally part and parcel of the Supreme Lord, and defines individuality as an eternal principle (2.12).

If we are all God, how is it that we are currently suffering in ignorance in this material world? What kind of God are we exactly? Does this not mean that ignorance or illusion is more powerful than God? How can that be?

The assertion that Krsna's form is material reveals a misunderstanding of His transcendental appearance and nature. Krsna's form is not composed of matter and possesses unlimited and diverse attributes. If Krsna is a mundane personality, why do self-realized souls feel drawn to hear about His pastimes (Bhag. 1.7.10)?

Reality is, as the mayavadis assert, beyond all material form and duality. However, they are mistaken in denying the existence of spiritual form and variety. The denial of variety is itself a material concept that does not provide us with positive information about spiritual reality.

Their desire to become one with God is rightly called the ultimate trap of maya. Frustrated in their attempts to be the supreme enjoyer of the material world, they wish to merge with the Supreme. This desire is an illusion because the soul has not realized its constitutional position as a servant of Krsna. The Srimad-Bhagavatam (10.2.32) points out that the misconception of the impersonalists arises from contaminated intelligence, and even if they attain a certain level of impersonal realization, they ultimately fall back to the material platform for failing to take shelter at the lotus feet of Krsna.

The material world is not false. Because it originates from the truth, it is real; however, the manifestations of this world are temporary. It is the belief that the material universe is permanent that is false.

questions.q_es: Señala que afirmación es correcta.
options_es: La ciencia actual mantiene la doctrina del origen de la vida basada en dogmas.
La ciencia actual tiene una postura correcta y basada en postulados científicos.
La ciencia actual no opina sobre temas que estan mas allá de la experimentación.
La ciencia actual no puede equivocarse, debido al consenso científico.
questions.q_en: What is the most effective way of preaching?

questions.q_es: ¿Qué es el reduccionismo?
options_es: La realidad es esencialmente simple y puede ser comprendida con el poder de nuestra mente y sentidos.
La tendencia de la ciencia a reducirlo todo a fórmulas matemáticas.
La experimentación con nanopartículas y material similar.
El hecho de que cuánto más preguntas menos sabes.
questions.q_en: What is the scientific evidence for the existence of God?
options_en: The design and order in nature proves an intelligent creator.

questions.q_es: ¿Cuáles son las consecuencias del reduccionismo?
options_es: Se pierden los valores, la ética y el significado de la vida.
No hay que estudiar tanto, ya que se prescinde de los detalles demasiado granulares.
No tiene ninguna consecuencia en absoluto.
questions.q_en: What is the relationship between Krsna and Christ?
options_en: Krsna and Christ come from the same root word 'Christos' meaning the anointed one.

questions.q_es: La palabra griega Christos significa el iluminado.
questions.q_en: What is the Darwinian theory of evolution?
options_en: That species evolve through natural selection.
That all species were created simultaneously.

questions.q_es: ¿Qué quiere decir la raíz de la palabra Krsna?
questions.q_en: What is the Vedic view on evolution?
options_en: The soul transmigrates through 8,400,000 species of life.

questions.q_es: ¿A qué corresponde el concepto del Espíritu Santo cristiano en la descripción de los Vedas?
questions.q_en: How should devotees deal with parents who oppose their spiritual practice?
options_en: By showing respect and demonstrating positive changes through their behavior.

questions.q_es: Hemos creado religiones cristianas, hindúes y musulmanas, pero cuando llegamos a una religión sin designaciones, en la que no pensamos que somos hindúes, cristianos o musulmanes, podemos hablar de religión pura o bhakti.
questions.q_en: What is the Mayavada philosophy?
options_en: The impersonal interpretation that everything is one and God has no form.

questions.q_es: ¿Qué es mukti?
questions.q_en: Who was the main proponent of Mayavada philosophy?

questions.q_es: La Verdad Absoluta se comprende en tres aspectos que son…
options_es: El estado de vigilia, de sueño y de sueño profundo.
questions.q_en: What is the difference between Mayavada and Vaisnavism?
options_en: Mayavada says God is impersonal; Vaisnavism says God is the Supreme Person.

questions.q_es: El tercer mandamiento cristiano es:
options_es: Amarás a tus hermanos, excluyendo a los animales.
questions.q_en: What is the best response when someone says 'all paths lead to God'?
options_en: Explain that while God is one, the processes to reach Him differ in effectiveness.

questions.q_es: ¿Porqué el devoto de Krsna se afeita la cabeza? De las 5, elige las 3 respuestas correctas.
options_es: Es la moda ahora y hay que seguir las modas de la sociedad para que les sea más fácil aceptarnos.
Es más limpio. E. Ayuda a liberarse de la vanidad y el orgullo.
questions.q_en: What is the soul?
options_en: An eternal spiritual particle, part and parcel of Krsna.

questions.q_es: Las mujeres, ¿son menos inteligentes? De las cuatro respuestas, dos son correctas.
options_es: En general las mujeres tienen mas dificultad en entender la vida espiritual.
Cuando una persona entiende que no es el cuerpo, se acaban los problemas y luchas de sexos.
No son menos inteligentes, son mas inteligentes y es un hecho demostrable.
El patriarcado ha sembrado la idea de que la mujer es menos inteligente para explotarla. Y ellas han caído en la trampa.
questions.q_en: What happens after death?
options_en: The soul transmigrates to another body according to its consciousness.

questions.q_es: Elige la frase que describe la vida espiritual devocional con mayor exactitud.
options_es: Todas las actividades relacionadas con el Señor son causa de felicidad espiritual.
Es una vida de austeridades y sacrificios para obtener al final el destino supremo de ir con Krsna.
Es una vida que te enseña a como se puede disfrutar de todo y ser realmente feliz.
questions.q_en: What is the purpose of human life?

questions.q_es: ¿Cómo puede un devoto renunciar a los placeres “normales” del mundo material?
options_es: Porque siente un placer mas elevado, el gusto superior.
Es cuestión de fuerza de voluntad que se adquiere con el bhakti yoga.
Se trata de la alimentación y los hábitos.
La compañía, todo se basa en con quien estás.
questions.q_en: How should a devotee respond to atheistic arguments?
options_en: With logic, scripture, and personal realization.

questions.q_es: ¿Porqué la conciencia de Krsna se dice que es una ciencia?
options_es: Por que puede ser probado con experimentación.
En realidad todo es cuestion de creencias.
No es una ciencia porque no hay objeto de experimentación.
Es una ciencia porque se estudian los voluminosos tratados de grandes sabios milenarios.
questions.q_en: What is the best way to introduce Krsna consciousness to newcomers?
options_en: Through prasadam, kirtana, and friendly association.
Through demanding they follow all rules immediately.

questions.q_es: Dios no tiene forma.
questions.q_en: What is the meaning of 'preaching'?

questions.q_es: ¿Porqué hay tantos problemas en la India si de ahi viene ésta cultura tan buena?
options_es: Los problemas vienen de No seguir la cultura.
Ahi me has pillado.  no sé que decir.
Los hindúes es que son así, siempre buscan problemas.
questions.q_en: What did Srila Prabhupada say about book distribution?
options_en: It is the most important preaching activity.

questions.q_es: Todas las entidades vivientes son una con Brahman, pero actualmente están cubiertas por la ilusión y, por lo tanto, separadas de Brahman. Cuando se disipa la ilusión, las entidades vivientes vuelven a ser una con Brahman y pierden su individualidad.
questions.q_en: Why is preaching important?
options_en: It is the best service to Krsna and the spiritual master.
It brings money to the temple.

questions.q_es: El mundo material es…
questions.q_en: What is the best qualification for preaching?
options_en: Sincerity and following the instructions of the spiritual master.

questions.q_es: ¿A qué se le llama la última trampa de maya?
options_es: El deseo de ser uno con Dios.
La mentira y el engaño en sí.
questions.q_en: How should a devotee deal with criticism?
options_en: Remain humble and depend on Krsna.

lessons.content_es:
FILOSOFÍA VÉDICA

 

Tres aspectos del Absoluto

Krsna y la Suprema Personalidad de Dios son idénticos. Por ello, se hace referencia al Señor Krsna como Bhagavan en todo el Gita. Bhagavan es lo último en la Verdad Absoluta. La Verdad Absoluta se realiza en tres fases de comprensión, a saber, Brahman, o el espíritu impersonal omnipresente; Paramatma, o el aspecto localizado del Supremo dentro del corazón de todas las entidades vivientes; y Bhagavan, o la Suprema Personalidad de Dios, el Señor Krsna. En el Srimad-Bhagavatam (1.2.11) esta concepción de la Verdad Absoluta se explica así:

«El conocedor de la Verdad Absoluta comprende la Verdad Absoluta en tres fases, y todas ellas son idénticas. Dichas fases de la Verdad Absoluta se expresan como Brahman, Paramatma y Bhagavan». Estos tres aspectos divinos pueden explicarse con el ejemplo del sol, que también tiene tres aspectos diferentes, a saber, la luz del sol, la superficie del sol y el planeta sol mismo. Quien solo estudia la luz del sol es el estudiante preliminar. Quien comprende la superficie del sol está más avanzado. Y el que puede entrar en el planeta sol es el más elevado. Los estudiantes comunes que están satisfechos simplemente entendiendo la luz del sol, su omnipresencia universal y la refulgencia deslumbrante de su naturaleza impersonal, se pueden comparar con aquellos que solo pueden darse cuenta de la característica Brahman de la Verdad Absoluta. El estudiante que ha avanzado aún más puede conocer el disco solar, que se compara con el conocimiento de la característica Paramatma de la Verdad Absoluta. Y el estudiante que puede entrar en el corazón del planeta solar se compara con aquellos que son conscientes de las características personales de la Suprema Verdad Absoluta.

Por lo tanto, los bhaktas, o los trascendentalistas que son conscientes de la característica Bhagavan de la Verdad Absoluta, son los trascendentalistas más importantes, aunque todos los estudiantes que participan en el estudio de la Verdad Absoluta están involucrados en la misma materia. La luz del sol, el disco solar y los asuntos internos del planeta solar no pueden separarse y sin embargo, los estudiantes de las tres fases diferentes no están en la misma categoría (Bhagavad-gita 2.2 significado).

El Todo Completo, o la Verdad Absoluta Suprema, es la Personalidad de Dios completa. La comprensión del Brahman impersonal o del Paramatma, la Superalma, es la realización incompleta del Absoluto Completo. La Suprema Personalidad de Dios es sac-cid-ananda-vigraha [Bs. 5.1]. La comprensión del Brahman impersonal es la realización de Su característica sat, o su aspecto de la eternidad, y la realización de Paramatma es la realización de sus características sat y cit, sus aspectos de eternidad y conocimiento. Pero la realización de la Personalidad de Dios es la realización de todas las características trascendentales: sat, cit y ananda, felicidad.

Cuando uno es consciente de la Persona Suprema, se da cuenta de estos aspectos de la Verdad Absoluta en su integridad. Vigraha significa «forma». Por lo tanto, el todo completo tiene forma. Si no tuviera forma, o si fuera menos que su creación de alguna otra manera, no podría ser completo. El todo completo debe contener todo, tanto dentro como más allá de nuestra experiencia; de lo contrario no puede ser completo (Sri Isopanisad, invocación, p.2).

 

 

Sanatana-dharma 

Sanatana-dharma no se refiere a ningún proceso sectario de religión. Es la función eterna de las entidades vivientes eternas en relación con el Señor Supremo eterno. Sanatana-dharma se refiere, como se dijo anteriormente, a la ocupación eterna de la entidad viviente. Sripada Ramanujacarya ha explicado la palabra sanatana como «aquello que no tiene principio ni fin«, por lo que cuando hablamos de sanatana-dharma, debemos dar por sentado, bajo la autoridad de Sripada Ramanujacarya, que no tiene principio ni fin.

La palabra religión es un poco diferente de sanatana-dharma. Religión transmite la idea de fe, y la fe puede cambiar. Uno puede tener fe en un proceso particular, y puede cambiar esa fe y adoptar otra, pero sanatana-dharma se refiere a esa actividad que no se puede cambiar. Por ejemplo, la liquidez no puede separarse del agua, ni el calor puede separarse del fuego. Del mismo modo, la función eterna de la entidad viviente eterna no se puede separar de la entidad viviente.

Sanatana-dharma es eternamente integral con la entidad viviente. Cuando hablamos de sanatana-dharma, por lo tanto, debemos dar por sentado con la autoridad de Sripada Ramanujacarya que no tiene principio ni fin. Lo que no tiene fin ni principio no puede ser sectario, ya que no puede estar restringido por ningún límite. Aquellos que pertenecen a alguna fe sectaria considerarán erróneamente que sanatana-dharma también es sectaria, pero si profundizamos en el asunto y lo consideramos a la luz de la ciencia moderna, es posible que veamos que sanatana-dharma es la ocupación de todas las personas del mundo, y es más, de todas las entidades vivientes del universo.

La fe religiosa no sanatana puede tener algún comienzo en los anales de la historia humana, pero la historia del sanatana-dharma no tiene principio, porque permanece eternamente con las entidades vivientes. En lo que respecta a las entidades vivientes, los sastras autorizados declaran que la entidad viviente no tiene nacimiento ni muerte. En el Gita se afirma que la entidad viviente nunca nace y nunca muere. Es eterna e indestructible, y continúa viviendo después de la destrucción de su cuerpo material temporal.

En referencia al concepto de sanatana-dharma, debemos tratar de entender el concepto de religión a partir del significado sánscrito de la palabra. Dharma se refiere a aquello que existe constantemente con un objeto particular. Concluimos que hay calor y luz junto con el fuego; sin calor y luz, la palabra fuego no tiene sentido. Del mismo modo, debemos descubrir la parte esencial del ser vivo, esa parte que es su compañero constante. Ese compañero constante es su cualidad eterna, y esa cualidad eterna es su religión eterna (Bhagavad-gita p.18-19).

 

Karmis, jnanis, yogis y bhaktas 

Sri Caitanya Mahaprabhu dijo: «Debido a que un devoto del Señor Krsna no tiene deseos, es pacífico. Los trabajadores fruitivos desean el disfrute material, los jnanis desean la liberación y los yogis desean la opulencia material; por lo tanto, todos son lujuriosos y no pueden ser pacíficos». SIGNIFICADO El devoto del Señor Krsna no tiene otro deseo que no sea servir a Krsna. Incluso las personas llamadas liberadas están llenas de deseos. Los actores fruitivos desean mejores destinos y los jnanis quieren ser uno con el Supremo. Los yogis desean la opulencia material, las perfecciones yóguicas y la magia. Todos estos no devotos son lujuriosos (kami). Porque desean algo, no pueden tener paz. Krsna da la fórmula de la paz en el Bhagavad-gita (5.29): bhoktaram yajna-tapasam sarva-loka-mahesvaram suhrdam sarva-bhutanam jnatva mam santim rcchati Aquel que simplemente entiende que Krsna es el disfrutador supremo de todo el universo y el beneficiario de todo tipo de sacrificios, penitencias y austeridades, que deben realizarse solo para lograr su servicio devocional, que Krsna es el Ser Supremo y, por lo tanto, el propietario de todos los mundos materiales. y que Krsna es el único amigo que realmente puede hacer el bien a todas las entidades vivientes (suhrdam sarva-bhutanam), alguien que entiende estos tres principios sobre Krsna se torna inmediatamente sin deseos (niskama) y, por lo tanto, pacífico. Un krsna-bhakta sabe que su amigo y protector en todos los aspectos es Krsna, quien puede hacer cualquier cosa por Su devoto. Krsna dice: kaunteya pratijanihi na me bhaktah pranasyati: «Oh, hijo de Kunti, declara audazmente que Mi devoto nunca perece». Dado que Krsna da esta garantía, el devoto vive en Krsna y no tiene ningún deseo de beneficio personal. El trasfondo para el devoto es el supremo bien mismo. ¿Por qué el devoto debe aspirar a algo bueno para sí mismo? Su única ocupación es complacer al Supremo prestando el mayor servicio posible. Un krsna-bhakta no desea su propio beneficio personal. Está completamente protegido por el Supremo. Avasya raksibe krsna visvasa palana. Bhaktivinoda Thakura dice que él no tiene deseos porque Krsna le protegerá en todas las circunstancias. No es que espere ayuda de Krsna; él simplemente depende de Krsna tal como un niño depende de sus padres. El niño no sabe cómo esperar el servicio de sus padres, pero siempre está protegido. Esto se llama niskama (falta de deseo). Aunque los karmis, los jnanis y los yogis cumplen sus deseos realizando diversas actividades, nunca están satisfechos. Un karmi puede trabajar muy duro para conseguir un millón de euros, pero tan pronto como recibe un millón desea otro millón. Para los karmis, el deseo no tiene fin. Cuanto más tiene el karmi, más desea. Los jnanis no pueden estar sin deseos porque su inteligencia no es sólida. Quieren fusionarse en la refulgencia de Brahman, pero a pesar de que pueden ser elevados a esa plataforma, no pueden estar satisfechos allí. Hay muchos jnanis o sannyasis que, después de tomar sannyasa y renunciar al mundo como falso, regresan al mundo para participar en política o filantropía o para abrir escuelas y hospitales. Esto significa que no pudieron alcanzar el verdadero Brahman (brahma satyam). Tienen que bajar a la plataforma material para participar en actividades filantrópicas. Así, nuevamente cultivan los deseos, y cuando estos deseos se agotan, desean algo diferente. Por lo tanto, el jnani no puede ser niskama, sin deseos. Los yogis tampoco pueden dejar de desear, ya que desean la perfección del yoga para exhibir algunas hazañas mágicas y ganar popularidad. La gente se reúne alrededor de estos yogis, y los yogis desean cada vez más adulación. Debido al mal uso de su poder místico, caen nuevamente a la plataforma material. No les es posible convertirse en niskama, sin deseos. La conclusión es que solo los devotos, que simplemente están satisfechos al servir al Señor, pueden volverse realmente sin deseos. Por lo tanto, Caitanya Mahaprabhu dice aquí, krsna-bhakta niskama. Como el krsna-bhakta, el devoto de Krsna, está satisfecho con Krsna, no hay posibilidad de caer (CC Madhya 19.149).

 

Karma 

La ciencia y la filosofía occidentales comúnmente sostienen que la ley de causa y efecto rige todas las acciones y eventos en el universo, que no puede haber acciones o eventos sin las causas correspondientes, al menos en la plataforma material. La literatura védica llama a esta ley de causa y efecto la ley del karma. Desde tiempos inmemoriales, el jiva ha estado actuando en el mundo material y disfrutando o sufriendo las reacciones de sus acciones. Sus acciones provocan su transmigración de un cuerpo material a otro. En otras palabras, el jiva se quita y se pone los cuerpos tal como uno se quita las prendas viejas e inútiles y se pone unas nuevas. A medida que el jiva transmigra, sufre o disfruta los resultados de sus actividades pasadas (karma). En cierto sentido, todo karma es esclavitud. Incluso las actividades piadosas, o «buen karma», atan a una persona a la rueda de la transmigración. Uno tiene que estar libre de todo karma para poder trascender el samsara, el nacimiento y la muerte repetidos. El jiva crea su propio karma a partir de sus deseos particulares de disfrutar el mundo de diferentes maneras. Por lo tanto, ni Bhagavan ni la naturaleza material son responsables del karma de la jiva, él labra su propio destino. Según las actividades de la jiva (y bajo la supervisión del Supremo), la naturaleza material simplemente le otorga a el jiva su próximo cuerpo para llevar a cabo sus deseos. La libertad de las cadenas del karma viene de la mano del conocimiento. «Así como el fuego abrasador convierte la leña en cenizas, oh Arjuna, así el fuego del conocimiento convierte en cenizas todas las reacciones (sarva karmani) a las actividades materiales». Ese «fuego del conocimiento» se refiere a la conciencia del jiva de su posición constitucional como el sirviente eterno del Supremo. Cuando uno se entrega a Bhagavan, termina todo el karma pasado, presente y futuro. El jiva no puede liberarse del karma simplemente por abstenerse de actuar. Los Vedas retratan el alma como eterna e irrevocablemente activa. De hecho, es imposible para un ser encarnado renunciar a todas las actividades. Por lo tanto, se dice que el que renuncia a los frutos de la acción (karma-phala-tyagi) es el que realmente ha renunciado. En otras palabras, uno tiene que aprender el arte de actuar sin acumular karma. En Bhagavad-gita, Bhagavan Sri Krsna explica este arte del karma-yoga con detalle. Brevemente, aquel que realiza sus actividades como sacrificio al Bhagavan Supremo, evita el karma, la esclavitud dentro del mundo material. Tal actividad refinada y de sacrificio se llama akarma, es decir, acción sin reacción. El Narada-Pancaratra explica que el arte del karma-yoga es hrsikena-hrsikesa-sevanam: «servir al Señor de los sentidos con los propios sentidos». La función del guru es enseñar a sus alumnos este elusivo arte del karma, acción sin reacción.

 

Jiva, el alma espiritual 

 

mamaivamso jiva-loke 

jiva-bhutah sanatanah 

manah-sasthanindriyani 

prakrti-sthani karsati 

«Las entidades vivientes en este mundo condicionado son Mis partes fragmentarias eternas. Debido a la vida condicionada, están luchando muy duro con los seis sentidos, que incluyen la mente». En este verso se explica claramente la identidad del ser vivo. La entidad viviente es la parte fragmentaria del Señor Supremo, eternamente. No es que asuma individualidad en su vida condicionada y en su estado liberado se convierta en uno con el Señor Supremo. Él es eternamente fragmentado. Se dice claramente, sanatanah. Según la versión védica, el Señor Supremo se manifiesta y se expande en innumerables expansiones, de las cuales las expansiones primarias se llaman visnu-tattva y las expansiones secundarias se llaman entidades vivientes. 

En otras palabras, el visnu-tattva es la expansión personal, y las entidades vivientes son las expansiones separadas. Mediante su expansión personal, se manifiesta en varias formas como el Señor Rama, Nrsimhadeva, Vishnumurti y todas las Deidades predominantes en los planetas Vaikuntha. Las expansiones separadas, las entidades vivientes, son eternamente servidores. Las expansiones personales de la Suprema Personalidad de Dios, las identidades individuales de la Deidad, siempre están presentes. Del mismo modo, las expansiones separadas de las entidades vivientes tienen sus identidades. Como partes y porciones fragmentarias del Señor Supremo, las entidades vivientes también tienen porciones fragmentarias de Sus cualidades, de las cuales la independencia es una. Cada entidad viviente, como alma individual, tiene su individualidad personal y una diminuta forma de independencia. Por el mal uso de esa independencia, uno se convierte en un alma condicionada, y por el uso apropiado de la independencia se libera. En cualquier caso, él es cualitativamente eterno, como lo es el Señor Supremo. En su estado liberado está liberado de esta condición material, y está bajo el compromiso del servicio trascendental al Señor; En su vida condicionada, está dominado por las modalidades materiales de la naturaleza, y olvida el servicio amoroso trascendental del Señor. Como resultado, tiene que luchar muy duro para mantener su existencia en el mundo material. Las entidades vivientes, no solo los seres humanos y los gatos y perros, sino incluso los mayores controladores del mundo material—Brahma, Shiva e incluso Vishnu—son partes integrales del Señor Supremo. Todos son manifestaciones eternas, no temporales. La palabra karsati («luchando» o «luchando duro«) es muy significativa. El alma condicionada está atada, como encadenada por cadenas de hierro. Está atado por el ego falso, y la mente es el principal agente que lo está impulsando en esta existencia material. Cuando la mente está en el modo de la bondad, sus actividades son buenas; cuando la mente está en el modo de la pasión, sus actividades son problemáticas; y cuando la mente está en el modo de ignorancia, viaja en las especies inferiores de la vida. Está claro, sin embargo, en este versículo, que el alma condicionada está cubierta por el cuerpo material, con la mente y los sentidos, y cuando se libera, esta cobertura material perece, pero su cuerpo espiritual se manifiesta en su capacidad individual. La siguiente información está allí en el Madhyandinayana-sruti que cuando una entidad viviente abandona esta encarnación material y entra en el mundo espiritual, revive su cuerpo espiritual, y en su cuerpo espiritual puede ver a la Suprema Personalidad de Dios cara a cara. Puede escucharle y hablarle cara a cara, y puede entender a la Suprema Personalidad tal como es. Del smrti también se comprende: vasanti yatra purusah sarve vaikuntha-murtayah: en los planetas espirituales todos viven en cuerpos semejantes al de la Suprema Personalidad de Dios. En lo que respecta a la construcción corporal, no hay diferencia entre las entidades vivientes parciales y las expansiones del visnu-murti. En otras palabras, en la liberación, la entidad viviente obtiene un cuerpo espiritual por la gracia de la Suprema Personalidad de Dios. Las palabras mamaivamsah («partes y porciones fragmentarios del Señor Supremo«) también son muy significativas. La porción fragmentaria del Señor Supremo no es como una parte material rota. Ya hemos entendido en el Segundo Capítulo que el espíritu no puede ser cortado en pedazos. Este fragmento no está materialmente concebido. No es como la materia, que se puede cortar en pedazos y volver a unir. Esa concepción no es aplicable aquí, porque se usa la palabra sánscrita sanatana («eterna«). La porción fragmentaria es eterna. También se afirma al comienzo del Segundo Capítulo que en cada cuerpo individual está presente la porción fragmentaria del Señor Supremo (dehino ’smin yatha dehe). Esa porción fragmentaria, cuando se libera del enredo corporal, revive su cuerpo espiritual original en el cielo espiritual en un planeta espiritual y disfruta de la compañía del Señor Supremo. Sin embargo, se entiende aquí que la entidad viviente, siendo la parte fragmentaria del Señor Supremo, es cualitativamente uno con el Señor, así como las partes y porciones de oro también son oro (Bhagavad gita 15.7).

 

 

 

Maya 

La ilusión material se llama maya. Maya significa «irrealidad, engaño, olvido«, «lo que no es». Bajo la influencia de maya, un hombre piensa que puede ser feliz dentro del mundo material temporal. Como la energía engañosa del Supremo, maya no actúa independientemente sino bajo su dirección. Es por ilusión (maya) que el jiva está confinado. Uno debería saber que la Naturaleza es ilusión (maya) y que el Señor Poderoso es el creador de la ilusión. El poder de Maya es tal que, aunque un hombre puede estar sufriendo múltiples miserias, se considerará feliz. «La causa del sufrimiento y la impotencia del hombre es maya, bajo cuya influencia olvida su naturaleza divina». Cuando el jiva se identifica con el cuerpo, desarrolla miles de deseos y luego intenta cumplirlos. Es la naturaleza del mundo material, que mas el jiva trata de explotar la situación material, más se ve comprometido por las complejidades de maya. Actuando bajo la influencia de maya, el jiva se somete a la ley del karma (causa y efecto). En cuanto al origen de maya, Bhagavan Krsna afirma: «Ésta energía divina mía (maya), que consta de los tres modos de la naturaleza material, es difícil de superar». Los Vedas afirman además: «Aunque maya (la ilusión) es falsa o temporal, el trasfondo de maya es el mago Supremo, la Personalidad de Dios, quién es Mahesvara, el controlador supremo».

En resumen, maya es una ilusión, un truco, un espejismo que desconcierta a una persona al pensar que la eternidad y la felicidad residen en las actividades del mundo material (que en realidad es temporal y miserable). Incluso un hombre altamente educado o inteligente puede estar bajo el hechizo de maya; el Bhagavad-gita designa a una persona tal como mayayapahrta-jnana, «alguien cuyo conocimiento es robado por maya». La literatura védica pretende liberar a todos los seres de la influencia de maya. Estar liberado de esta ilusión que de alguna manera ha llegado a dominar la raza humana, es el fin de todo esfuerzo. Según el Bhagavad-gita, es muy difícil para el jiva liberarse de la esclavitud de maya: «Esta energía divina mía, que consta de los tres modos de la naturaleza material, es difícil de superar. Pero aquellos que se han entregado a Mí, pueden cruzarla fácilmente».

Tres modalidades de la naturaleza material 

Las tres modalidades de la naturaleza material son la bondad (sattvam), la pasión (rajas) y la ignorancia (tamas). Son las fuerzas de control que gobiernan todos los objetos y actividades dentro del mundo material. Las personas en el modo de ignorancia están controladas por el sueño, la locura, la intoxicación, la pereza, la inmundicia, etc. Las personas apasionadas están controladas por un deseo intenso y anhelos, especialmente simbolizados por el deseo sexual. Los que están en la bondad poseen síntomas de felicidad, tranquilidad e iluminación. La bondad es el trampolín hacia la vida espiritual. En la medida en que las actividades devocionales son materiales, están en el modo de la bondad. La posición trascendental es de pura bondad, donde uno ya no está influenciado por los modos de la naturaleza material. Krsna dice que aquellos que se entregan a Él pueden cruzar fácilmente los modos de la naturaleza. De lo contrario, todos están completamente bajo el control de estos modos a pesar de que se creen libres.

 

Cinco tipos de liberación 

Un devoto puro no acepta ningún tipo de liberación: salokya, sarsti, samipya, sarupya o ekatva, a pesar de que la Suprema Personalidad de Dios se los ofrece. 

SIGNIFICADO 

Aquí se establece claramente que un devoto puro no desea ekatva, unidad con el Señor Supremo, como lo desean los impersonalistas, los especuladores mentales y los meditadores. Convertirse en uno con el Señor Supremo está más allá del sueño de un devoto puro. De alguna manera puede aceptar el ascenso a los planetas Vaikuntha para servir al Señor allí, pero nunca aceptará fusionarse con la refulgencia de Brahman, que considera peor que infernal. Tal ekatva, o fusionarse en la refulgencia del Señor Supremo, se llama kaivalya, pero el devoto puro considera que la felicidad derivada de kaivalya es infernal. El devoto es tan aficionado a prestar servicio al Señor Supremo que los cinco tipos de liberación no son importantes para él. Si uno se dedica al servicio amoroso trascendental puro al Señor, se entiende que ya ha logrado los cinco tipos de liberación. Cuando un devoto es promovido al mundo espiritual, Vaikuntha, recibe cuatro tipos de beneficios. Uno de ellos es salokya, que es vivir en el mismo planeta que la Suprema Personalidad. La Persona Suprema, en sus diferentes expansiones plenarias, vive en innumerables planetas Vaikuntha, y el planeta principal es Krsnaloka. Así como dentro del universo material, el planeta principal es el sol, en el mundo espiritual el planeta principal es Krsnaloka. Desde Krsnaloka, la refulgencia corporal del Señor Krsna se distribuye no solo al mundo espiritual sino también al mundo material. Sin embargo, está cubierto por la materia en el mundo material. En el mundo espiritual hay innumerables planetas Vaikuntha, y en cada uno de ellos el Señor es la Deidad predominante. 

Un devoto puede ser promovido a uno de esos planetas Vaikuntha para vivir con la Suprema Personalidad de Dios. En la liberación sarsti, la opulencia del devoto es igual a la opulencia del Señor Supremo. Samipya significa ser un asociado personal del Señor Supremo. En la liberación de sarupya, los rasgos corporales del devoto son exactamente como los de la Persona Suprema, excepto dos o tres que se encuentran exclusivamente en el cuerpo trascendental del Señor. Srivatsa, por ejemplo, unos cabellos en el pecho del Señor, lo distinguen particularmente de Sus devotos. Un devoto puro no acepta esos cinco tipos de opulencias espirituales, incluso si se les ofrece, y ciertamente no anhela los beneficios materiales, que son insignificantes en comparación con los beneficios espirituales. 

Cuando a Prahlada Maharaja se le ofreció algún beneficio material, declaró: «Mi Señor, he visto que mi padre logró todo tipo de beneficios materiales, e incluso los semidioses temieron su opulencia, pero aún así, en un segundo, has terminado con su vida y toda su prosperidad material». Para un devoto no se trata de desear ninguna prosperidad material o espiritual. Él simplemente aspira a servir al Señor. Esa es su mayor felicidad (Srimad Bhagavatam 3.29.13).

 

Acintya-bhedabheda tattva 

Los filósofos impersonalistas de la escuela Mayavada creen que el Supremo y la entidad viviente son lo mismo. En consecuencia, se consideran Dios. Sin embargo, dicen que el mundo cósmico es una creación de maya y, por lo tanto, es falso. Esto se explica con la declaración brahma satyam jagat mithya: «Brahman (o espíritu) es verdadero y el mundo de la materia es falso». La filosofía vaisnava es acinty-abheda-abheda tattva, lo que significa que simultáneamente todo es uno con Krsna, pero es diferente. Todo es uno porque todo es energía de Krsna, por ello, no decimos que la materia es falsa, sino que decimos que es temporal. Así pues, aunque la energía de Krsna está en todas partes, Krsna permanece en Su característica personal como la Fuente de energía independiente.

 

Tres energías de Krsna 

Se menciona que las potencias primarias de la Verdad Absoluta son tres: internas, externas y marginales. Por los actos de su potencia interna, la Personalidad de Dios en su forma original exhibe las manifestaciones cósmicas espirituales conocidas como Vaikunthalokas, que existen eternamente, incluso después de la destrucción de la manifestación cósmica material. Mediante su potencia marginal, el Señor se expande a sí mismo como seres vivos que son parte de él, así como el sol distribuye sus rayos en todas las direcciones. Mediante su potencia externa, el Señor manifiesta la creación material, así como el sol crea la niebla con sus rayos. La creación material no es más que un reflejo desvirtuado de la naturaleza eterna de Vaikuntha. Estas tres energías de la Verdad Absoluta también se describen en el Vishnu Purana, donde se dice que el ser vivo es igual en calidad a la potencia interna, mientras que la potencia externa está indirectamente controlada por la causa principal de todas las causas. Maya, la energía ilusoria, engaña a un ser vivo, así como la niebla nos engaña y creemos que realmente bloquea la luz del sol. Aunque la potencia de maya es inferior en calidad a la potencia marginal, que consiste en los seres vivos, que son parte integrantes del Señor, sin embargo tiene el poder de controlar a los seres vivos, así como la niebla puede bloquear las acciones de un cierta porción de los rayos del sol aunque no puede cubrir el sol. Los seres vivos cubiertos por la energía ilusoria evolucionan en diferentes especies de vida, con cuerpos que van desde el de una insignificante hormiga hasta el de Brahma, el constructor del cosmos. El pradhana, la causa principal de todas las causas en la visión impersonal, no es otro que el Señor Supremo, a quien uno puede ver cara a cara en la potencia interna. Él toma la forma material omnipresente mediante su poder inconcebible. Aunque las tres potencias, es decir, interna, externa y marginal, son esencialmente las principales, son diferentes en acción, de la misma forma en que la energía eléctrica puede producir frío y calor bajo diferentes condiciones. 

Las potencias externas y marginales se denominan así bajo diversas condiciones, pero en las potencias internas originales no existen tales condiciones, ni es posible que las condiciones de la potencia externa existan en la marginal, o viceversa. Quien es capaz de comprender las complejidades de todas estas energías del Señor Supremo ya no puede seguir siendo un impersonalista empírico bajo la influencia de una escasa reserva de conocimiento (CC Adi, 2.96).
lessons.content_en:
Vedic Philosophy

Three Aspects of the Absolute

Krsna and the Supreme Personality of God are identical. Therefore, the Lord Krsna is referred to as Bhagavan throughout the Gita. Bhagavan is the ultimate in Absolute Truth. The Absolute Truth is realized in three phases of understanding, namely, Brahman, or the omnipresent impersonal spirit; Paramatma, or the localized aspect of the Supreme within the heart of all living entities; and Bhagavan, or the Supreme Personality of God, Lord Krsna. In the Srimad-Bhagavatam (1.2.11), this conception of Absolute Truth is explained as follows:

"The knower of the Absolute Truth understands the Absolute Truth in three phases, and all of them are identical. These phases of the Absolute Truth are expressed as Brahman, Paramatma, and Bhagavan." These three divine aspects can be illustrated with the example of the sun, which also has three different aspects: the sunlight, the surface of the sun, and the sun planet itself. Those who study only the sunlight are the preliminary students. Those who understand the surface of the sun are more advanced. And those who can enter the sun planet are the most elevated. Common students who are satisfied merely by understanding the sunlight, its universal omnipresence, and the dazzling brilliance of its impersonal nature can be compared to those who can only realize the Brahman feature of the Absolute Truth. The student who has advanced further can know the solar disc, which is compared to the knowledge of the Paramatma feature of the Absolute Truth. And the student who can enter the heart of the sun planet is compared to those who are aware of the personal characteristics of the Supreme Absolute Truth.

Therefore, the bhaktas, or transcendentalists who are aware of the Bhagavan feature of the Absolute Truth, are the most important transcendentalists, although all students engaged in the study of the Absolute Truth are involved in the same subject. The sunlight, the solar disc, and the internal affairs of the sun planet cannot be separated, yet the students of the three different phases are not in the same category (Bhagavad-gita 2.2 purport).

The Complete Whole, or the Supreme Absolute Truth, is the complete Personality of God. The understanding of the impersonal Brahman or the Paramatma, the Supersoul, is the incomplete realization of the Complete Absolute. The Supreme Personality of God is sac-cid-ananda-vigraha [Bs. 5.1]. The understanding of the impersonal Brahman is the realization of His sat feature, or His aspect of eternity, and the realization of Paramatma is the realization of His sat and cit features, His aspects of eternity and knowledge. But the realization of the Personality of God is the realization of all the transcendental features: sat, cit, and ananda, bliss.

When one is aware of the Supreme Person, one realizes these aspects of the Absolute Truth in their entirety. Vigraha means "form." Therefore, the complete whole has form. If it did not have form, or if it were less than its creation in some way, it could not be complete. The complete whole must contain everything, both within and beyond our experience; otherwise, it cannot be complete (Sri Isopanisad, invocation, p.2).

Sanatana-dharma

Sanatana-dharma does not refer to any sectarian process of religion. It is the eternal function of eternal living entities in relation to the eternal Supreme Lord. Sanatana-dharma refers, as mentioned earlier, to the eternal occupation of the living entity. Sripada Ramanujacarya has explained the word sanatana as "that which has no beginning or end," so when we speak of sanatana-dharma, we must assume, under the authority of Sripada Ramanujacarya, that it has no beginning or end.

The word religion is somewhat different from sanatana-dharma. Religion conveys the idea of faith, and faith can change. One may have faith in a particular process, and that faith can change and adopt another, but sanatana-dharma refers to that activity which cannot be changed. For example, liquidity cannot be separated from water, nor can heat be separated from fire. Similarly, the eternal function of the eternal living entity cannot be separated from the living entity.

Sanatana-dharma is eternally integral with the living entity. Therefore, when we speak of sanatana-dharma, we must assume with the authority of Sripada Ramanujacarya that it has no beginning or end. What has no end or beginning cannot be sectarian, as it cannot be restricted by any limits. Those who belong to some sectarian faith may mistakenly consider sanatana-dharma to be sectarian as well, but if we delve into the matter and consider it in light of modern science, we may see that sanatana-dharma is the occupation of all people in the world, and moreover, of all living entities in the universe.

Non-sanatana religious faith may have some beginning in the annals of human history, but the history of sanatana-dharma has no beginning, because it eternally exists with living entities. Regarding living entities, the authorized sastras declare that the living entity has no birth or death. In the Gita, it is stated that the living entity never takes birth and never dies. It is eternal and indestructible, and continues to live after the destruction of its temporary material body.

In reference to the concept of sanatana-dharma, we must try to understand the concept of religion from the Sanskrit meaning of the word. Dharma refers to that which constantly exists with a particular object. We conclude that there is heat and light along with fire; without heat and light, the word fire has no meaning. Similarly, we must discover the essential part of the living being, that part which is its constant companion. That constant companion is its eternal quality, and that eternal quality is its eternal religion (Bhagavad-gita pp. 18-19).

Karmis, jnanis, yogis, and bhaktas

Sri Caitanya Mahaprabhu said: "Because a devotee of Lord Krsna has no desires, he is peaceful. Fruitive workers desire material enjoyment, jnanis desire liberation, and yogis desire material opulence; therefore, all of them are lusty and cannot be peaceful." MEANING The devotee of Lord Krsna has no other desire than to serve Krsna. Even those who are called liberated are filled with desires.
