Visarga, or secondary creation, refers to the appearance of Lord Brahma and his creation of the varieties of planets and species within the universe using the ingredients manifested during the sarga phase. The sarga phase of creation is described as follows. From the very energy of Lord Krsna, mahat-tattva, or total material energy, is produced, from maha-tattva, the false ego produces three different aspects: false ego in goodness, false ego in passion, and false ego in ignorance.

These three different aspects arise from the influence of the three modes of material nature on the false ego. From the false ego in goodness, the mind is generated and manifested, along with the ten demigods that control bodily movements. Through the transformation of the false ego in passion, intelligence is born. The ego in the mode of passion also produces two types of senses: the senses for acquiring knowledge, such as hearing, touch, smell, sight, and taste, and the senses of action, such as the mouth, hands, legs, genitals, and the outlet for evacuation. When the ego in ignorance is agitated, the subtle element of sound manifests, and from sound, the ether and the sense of hearing arise. From ether or sky evolve the subtle elements of touch, and from there, air and the sense of touch emerge.

From air arises the evolution of form, the element fire, and the sense of sight. Through the interaction of fire and visual sensation, the subtle element of taste develops. From taste, water is produced, along with the tongue. The subtle element of smell evolves next, leading to earth and the sense of smell. From the previous description, it is clear that the Vedic elements are connected with the senses of the jivatma and also with the objects of the senses. As mentioned earlier, Europeans until the late 18th century also believed that all matter was composed of earth, water, fire, and air. Unfortunately, however, they did not understand the subtle nature of these elements as described in the Srimad-Bhagavatam; therefore, they took the element water as ordinary water, the element air as the ordinary air we breathe, and so forth. The Vedic account refers to something much more sophisticated.

It is by no means a primitive account. It refers to an area where science has not advanced until now, as scientists cannot comprehend how consciousness relates to matter.

**Avatars**

The avatar, or incarnation of God, descends from the kingdom of God to the material manifestation. The particular form of the Personality of God that thus descends is called an incarnation or avatar. Such incarnations are situated in the spiritual world, the kingdom of God. When they descend to material creation, they take on the name of avatar. There are various types of avatars, such as purusavataras, gunavataras, mavataras, saktyavesa-avataras, manvantara-avataras, and yugavataras, all appearing in an order throughout the universe. But Lord Krsna is the Supreme Lord, the source of all avatars. Lord Krsna descends with the specific purpose of alleviating the anxieties of pure devotees, who are very eager to see Him in His original pastimes in Vrndavana. Therefore, the main purpose of Krsna's avatar is to satisfy His pure devotees.

No one should be accepted as an avatar unless the Scriptures refer to him. It is not a fact that the Lord appears only on Hindu soil. He can manifest anywhere and everywhere, whenever He wishes to appear. In each and every incarnation, He speaks of religion as can be understood by people in particular circumstances. But the mission is the same: to bring people to God consciousness and to obedience to the principles of religion. He personally descends and sometimes sends His genuine representative in the form of His son, or servant, or He Himself in some disguised form. The principles of the Bhagavad-gita were spoken to Arjuna and, in reality, to other very elevated individuals, because he was very advanced compared to ordinary people from other parts of the world.

Two plus two equals four is a mathematical principle that is true both in beginner arithmetic and in advanced classes. Still, there are higher and more basic mathematics. In all the incarnations of the Lord, therefore, the same principles are taught, but they seem to be higher or lower in various circumstances. The higher principles of religion begin with the acceptance of the four orders and the four states of social life, as will be explained later. The entire purpose of the missions of the incarnations is to awaken Krsna consciousness everywhere. Such consciousness is manifested and unmanifested only under different circumstances (Bhagavad-gita 4.8 and 4.7, meanings).

**Lila—avatars and other incarnations**

The incarnations of Krsna are as numerous as the waves of the ocean, but there are ten Lila avatars described in the Srimad-Bhagavatam. These incarnations appear in extraordinary circumstances and perform unimaginable pastimes.

**Matsya**

is the Lord's fish incarnation, which appeared twice. First, He appeared during the reign of Caksusa Manu to protect the pious king Satyavrata. Lord Matsya first appeared as a small fish but grew to a gigantic size. During a partial flood of the universe, King Satyavrata joined the fish that pulled him and seven rsis. Secondly, in the ocean of dissolution, Lord Matsya saved the Vedas in a boat that He steered.

**Kurma**

was Krsna's incarnation as a tortoise. The demigods and demons wanted to churn the ocean of milk to extract nectar. They used Mount Mandara as a pivot and the serpent Vasuki as a rope for churning, while Lord Kurma appeared to support the mountain on His back. The Lord accepted that churning as a way to scratch His back and felt pleased.

**Varaha**

, or Lord Boar, seemed to lift the planet earth on His tusks when it had fallen into the Garbodhaka ocean due to the nefarious activities of Hiranyaksa. Appearing from the nostril of Lord Brahma, Lord Varaha grew to a gigantic size, rescued the earth, and then killed Hiranyaksa, the first king of the demons.

**Nrsimhadeva**

was the incarnation of the Lord as a half-man, half-lion being. He appeared to protect His devotee Prahlada from the malice of his father, the demoniac king Hiranyakasipu, the twin brother of Hiranyaksa. Lord Nrsimhadeva is the protector of all the Lord's devotees, and every day they worship Him.

**Vamanadeva**

, the incarnation of a dwarf brahmana, appeared to help the demigods reclaim their kingdom from the conqueror Bali Maharaja. Lord Vamanadeva begged for three steps of land from Bali Maharaja, who thought he was giving charity to an ordinary brahmana. With His first step, Lord Vamanadeva covered the entire planet, and with the second, He covered the entire universe, piercing a hole in its limit through which the Ganges flowed. Upon seeing that he had lost his entire kingdom, Bali Maharaja asked the Lord to place His third step on his head. Bali Maharaja was first sent to hell because he could not provide the three promised steps of land, but the Lord later granted him a planet, Sutala, and He personally became its gatekeeper.

**Parasurama**

was Krsna's incarnation as a ksatriya. He appeared to rid the world of the demoniac ruling class and killed the ksatriya community twenty-one times.

**Ramacandra**

was Krsna's incarnation as the ideal king. He was exiled to the forest with his wife Sita and his brother Laksmana due to the evil desires of his stepmother. There he remained for twelve years and ultimately killed the demoniac king Ravana, who had kidnapped Sita. After his exile, he returned with Sita and became the king of Ayodhya, to the great delight of all the citizens.

**Buddha**

---

Being compassionate towards innocent animals, He expounded the philosophy of Ahimsa, non-violence, to protect the poor animals that were being indiscriminately sacrificed under the pretext of following Vedic rituals. Lord Buddha deceived atheists by denouncing the Vedas and making them worship Him.

**Balarama**

is a svamsa expansion of the Lord, and therefore, there is no difference in potency between Krsna and Balarama. The only difference lies in their bodily structures. As the first expansion of God, Balarama is the principal Deity among the first quadruple forms and is the chief assistant of Sri Krsna in His transcendental activities.

**Kalki**

is the incarnation of Krsna who will appear at the end of Kali yuga to annihilate all. It is explained that people will be so demoniac that there will be no opportunity for preaching. Therefore, Krsna will kindly cut off the heads of all with a sword while riding a white horse, thus liberating all people.

**Other Incarnations**

At the beginning of creation, the Lord expanded in the universal form of the **Purusa-avataras**. The first three incarnations are Karanodakasayi-Vishnu or Maha-Vishnu. From the pores of His skin, countless universes have emerged. In each universe, the second incarnation appears as Garbhodakasayi-Vishnu. He lies on the Garbhodaka ocean, which is filled with the water of His own sweat. From His navel grows the stem of the lotus flower, which is the birthplace of Lord Brahma. Within the stem of the lotus, there are fourteen divisions of planetary systems. From Garbhodakasayi-Vishnu comes Ksirodakasayi-Vishnu, who is the collective Paramatma or Supersoul of all living beings. He is called Hari, and from Him, all the incarnations that appear in this universe expand.

The Lord appeared as **Kapila Muni**, the son of Devahuti and Kardama. He instructed His mother on self-realization in the form of Sankhya philosophy, as narrated in the Third Canto of the Srimad-Bhagavatam.

The Lord Himself incarnated as Lord **Vishnu** for the maintenance of the material world. Lord Brahma was born from the navel of Garbhodakasayi Vishnu for the purpose of creation, and Lord Shiva was born from the anger of Lord Brahma for the purpose of universal destruction. They take charge of the modes of goodness, passion, and ignorance, respectively, and are known as guna avataras. At the beginning of creation, there were four sons of Lord Brahma known as **Kumaras**. They wished never to grow up and, therefore, were never affected by sexual desire. They appear as five-year-old children. They taught the process of realizing Brahman and underwent severe penances. Later, they became devotees by smelling the incense and Tulasi leaves offered to the Lord.

The Lord appeared as **Dattatreya**, the son of the great sage Atri Muni and his wife Anasuya. The sage's wife prayed to Lord Brahma, Lord Vishnu, and Lord Shiva to combine and become her son. The Lord agreed to do so and, as Dattatreya, appeared to expound the philosophy of the spiritual soul.

The Lord incarnated as **Nara and Narayana Rsis**, the twin sons of King Dharma. They underwent severe and exemplary penances to control their senses. Their Lordships are worshiped in Badari Narayana Ashrama in the Himalayas. Once, Cupid tried to break Their vows of austerity by presenting celestial beauties before Them, but They manifested millions of women even more beautiful than the demigods sent by Cupid.

The Lord appeared as **Suyajna**, the son of Prajapati Ruci and his wife Akuti. During the reign of Svayambhuva Manu, there was no one to accept the position of King Indra to manage universal affairs, so the Lord agreed to accept that position and ruled with His sons.

He also appeared as King **Rsabha**, the son of King Nabhi and Merudevi. This incarnation showed the path of perfection followed by those who fully control their senses. He had one hundred sons, headed by Maharaja Bharata. He gave very elaborate instructions (referred to in the Fifth Canto of the Srimad-Bhagavatam) on how to free oneself from material bondage.

King **Prthu** was an incarnation of Lord Krsna who appeared (by the prayers of the great sages) from the body of King Vena. He accepted the role of an ideal king and cultivated the land to produce many crops and products. He also performed ninety-nine horse sacrifices, and the citizens were extremely happy under His rule.

The Lord appeared as **Dhanvantari**, a plenary portion of a plenary portion of Lord Vishnu. He emerged from the ocean of milk and inaugurated the science of medicine in the universe. This science is the basis of Ayur Veda.

The incarnation of the Lord as **Srila Vyasadeva** appeared in the womb of Satyavati, through Parasara Muni. He divided the original Veda into four: Sama, Yajur, Rg, and Atharva. He then explained them in different branches such as the Puranas and the Mahabharata. Vyasadeva did this for the benefit of less intelligent people in the age of Kali.

**Sri Caitanya Mahaprabhu**, the Golden Avatar Five hundred years ago, the Supreme Personality of Godhead appeared as Sri Caitanya Mahaprabhu, the Golden Avatar. Lord Caitanya is the most recent and merciful incarnation of God and is known as Patita-pavana, the savior of the most fallen. He appeared in Bengal in 1486 in the province of Nadia in Sridhama Mayapura, Navadvipa, and there inaugurated the sankirtana movement to propagate the chanting of the maha-mantra Hare Krsna Hare Krsna Krsna Krsna Hare Hare / Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Lord Caitanya exhibited wonderful pastimes and demonstrated Himself to be the Personality of Godhead. His advent was predicted in the revealed scriptures, most notably in the Srimad-Bhagavatam (11.5.32).

It is stated there that the Supreme Personality of Godhead will appear in His devotional aspect, accompanied by His associates, to present the yuga-dharma, the chanting of the holy name of the Lord. It also mentions that those who have sufficient intelligence will worship the Lord in this fallen age of Kali by participating in the sankirtana movement. That Sri Caitanya Mahaprabhu was an incarnation of God was clear to many saintly persons, especially to Rupa Gosvami, who recited the following verse upon meeting the Lord in this prayer: "My dear Lord, You are the most generous incarnation of God. And the proof is that You are distributing what no one else, not even Krsna Himself, has distributed before, namely, pure love for Krsna."

The Absolute Truth is one, but it became two as Radha and Krsna, and now it has become one again as Sri Caitanya Mahaprabhu. Lord Caitanya is Krsna Himself, appearing with the complexion of His eternal consort, Srimati Radharani, to experience the feelings of separation of Radharani from Himself. This mood is further expressed in the prayers of the Siksastaka of Lord Caitanya in the following way: "Oh, Govinda, feeling Your separation, I consider a moment to be like twelve years or more; tears flow from my eyes like torrents of rain, and I feel that everything is empty in this world in Your absence." To experience how the love of His devotees always increases due to separation, Krsna appeared as His own devotee, as Sri Caitanya Mahaprabhu.

Porque la Deidad posee el principio de atracción total.

En realidad, no tiene significado concreto.

Digno de adoración, santo, divino, sagrado e inmortal, tenido en alta estima.
Rico, poderoso, famoso, hermoso, sabio y desapegado.

En Goloka está Krsna y en Gokula solo Sus devotos.

questions.q_en: Who is Lord Rama?
options_en: The incarnation of the Lord who appeared as the perfect king.

questions.q_es: ¿Qué dos fases de la creación se describen en la literatura védica?
questions.q_en: What is Goloka Vrndavana?
options_en: The supreme abode of Lord Krsna.
A planet in the material world.

questions.q_es: ¿Qué es el mahat-tattva?
questions.q_en: How many material universes are there?

questions.q_es: ¿Qué es un avatar?
options_es: Un personaje de una pelicula famosa.
Dios cuando desciende a este mundo.
questions.q_en: What is the duration of Brahma's day?
options_en: One thousand cycles of four yugas (4.32 billion years).

questions.q_es: ¿Cuál es el criterio para reconocer a un avatar?
options_es: Que aparezca referido en las Escrituras.
Si un suficiente número de gente lo reconoce.
Si no hay pruebas de lo contrario.
questions.q_en: What happens at the end of Brahma's day?

questions.q_es: ¿Hace cuanto tiempo apareció Sri Caitanya Mahaprabhu?
questions.q_en: What is the mahat-tattva?
options_en: The total material energy from which everything is created.

questions.q_es: ¿Con qué propósito principal apareció Sri Caitanya Mahaprabhu?
options_es: Para expandir el movimiento de sankirtana.
Para experimentar cómo el amor de Sus devotos siempre aumenta debido a la separación.
Para derrotar a los filósofos impersonalistas.
questions.q_en: How many species of life are there?

lessons.content_es:
CULTURA VÉDICA

 

Definición de hinduismo

Cuando hablamos de conciencia de Krsna, no debemos confundirlo con el hinduismo. La conciencia de Krsna se origina en las antiguas Escrituras védicas; el hinduismo no se menciona en absoluto en los Vedas. Además, el uso popular actual del término «hinduismo» no corresponde a su significado original.

La palabra «hindú» es una mala pronunciación persa de «sindhu«, el nombre sánscrito del río Indo. Para los invasores Mogules, que entraron en la India cruzando el Indo, era natural referirse al territorio conquistado como la Tierra del Río Indo, o Indostán. Así, un «hindú» es, hablando con propiedad, un habitante del Indostán, independientemente de su religión.

Los británicos, sin embargo, usaron «hindú» para referirse a los indios que no eran musulmanes, budistas, sijs, jainistas o miembros de otros grupos religiosos para los que los británicos tenían nombres. «Hindú» se usó como un todo para describir literalmente a miles de patrones religiosos y culturales bastante diferentes en la inmensa área del subcontinente indio. Por lo tanto, hablar de «hinduismo» es tan engañoso que carece de sentido. Nuestra práctica, de acuerdo con la enseñanza de las escrituras védicas, se llama propiamente sanatana-dharma, que se traduce mejor como «ley eterna». Sin embargo, para el uso ordinario, Srila Prabhupada nos ha enseñado que el nombre más claro e informativo para nuestra práctica es conciencia de Krsna.

Varnasrama-dharma, el sistema social védico

Varnasrama-dharma es el sistema social védico que divide a la sociedad en dos conjuntos de cuatro divisiones, el primer conjunto sobre la base de la ocupación y el segundo sobre la base de la posición espiritual (asrama).

Los cuatro varnas (ocupaciones) son los brahmanas, que son los intelectuales; los ksatriyas, guerreros y administradores; los vaisyas, agricultores y comerciantes; y los sudras, trabajadores a sueldo. El lugar de una persona dentro de este sistema es determinado por sus cualidades y actividades, no por su nacimiento, y debe recibir educación para calificarse para un puesto en particular. Los deberes de los cuatro varnas pueden resumirse brevemente como sigue:

Los 

brahmanas

 son maestros que enseñan tanto con el ejemplo como con el precepto. Deben conocer los principios morales y ser autocontrolados. También sirven al guiar a los ksatriyas para garantizar que todas las personas sigan los principios religiosos.

Los 

ksatriyas

 administran de acuerdo con las instrucciones recibidas de los brahmanas y las Escrituras. Deben ser completamente religiosos. Se les conoce como «naradeva» o representantes de Dios en la tierra. Si hay una batalla, dirigen personalmente las fuerzas militares.

Los 

vaisyas

 están a cargo de la agricultura, el comercio y la protección de las vacas. Dado que el propósito de la sociedad es la conducción de una vida espiritual simple, no hay producción ni comercio de bienes para una vida excesivamente lujosa y actividades pecaminosas.

Los 

sudras

 son la clase obrera y prestan servicio ayudando a las otras tres clases.

El cuerpo social se compara con el cuerpo humano, con los brahmanas como cabeza, los ksatriyas como brazos, los vaisyas como estómago y los sudras como piernas. Este es un sistema natural para dividir el trabajo, no un sistema de castas artificial. Dentro de este sistema, todos se elevan gradualmente a la realización espiritual bajo la guía de los brahmanas, en un sistema social que satisface las necesidades reales de todos sus miembros sin perturbar su progreso espiritual.

Los cuatro asramas representan etapas dentro de la vida de un individuo, a medida que crece en conciencia espiritual y renuncia. Son brahmacarya o vida estudiantil; grhastha o vida matrimonial; vanaprastha, o vida de retirado y sannyasa, o la orden de vida de renuncia.

Uno se convierte en brahmacari a la edad de cinco años y vive con el maestro espiritual hasta los veinticinco. Recibe educación tanto espiritual como material, por lo que está preparado para comprender la necesidad de desarrollar la conciencia espiritual a lo largo de su vida. Simultáneamente, se le forma en su varna u ocupación.

A los veinticinco años, el brahmacari puede dejar el gurukula y tomar una esposa. Su entrenamiento le convertirá en un esposo calificado capaz de guiar a su familia hacia la realización espiritual. A los cincuenta años se retira de la vida familiar y visita lugares santos con su esposa; eso es vanaprastha. A la edad de setenta y cinco años, confía el cuidado de su esposa a su hijo mayor y adopta sannyasa, preparándose completamente para regresar a Dios cantando constantemente el nombre del Señor y sirviéndole sin ninguna otra ocupación.

 

Preguntas y respuestas sobre la mujer en conciencia de Krsna.

(por Visakha-devi dasi)

¿Por qué deberían las mujeres estar interesadas en la conciencia de Krsna?

¡Para darse cuenta de que no son mujeres! Al identificarnos como «mujeres«, revelamos la ignorancia de nuestra identidad real. La palabra «mujer» se refiere al cuerpo material temporal, no al alma espiritual eterna, la persona dentro del cuerpo. En tal vez cuarenta o sesenta años el cuerpo que ahora llevo perecerá y ya no seré una mujer. Pero la fuerza viviente, el alma dentro del cuerpo no perece. La conciencia de Krsna nos dota del entendimiento de la relación del alma con la materia y con el Alma Suprema, Krsna. La inteligencia humana sirve para meditar en esos temas, no para morar en pensamientos materialistas basados en la falsa identificación con el cuerpo temporal.

¿Qué opinas del movimiento de liberación de la mujer?

La liberación más elevada es regresar a casa, regresar a Dios y nunca más enfrentar las miserias del nacimiento, la muerte, la vejez, la enfermedad y la muerte. Aunque el movimiento de liberación de la mujer puede ofrecer algunas propuestas valiosas, generalmente ignora este objetivo supremo. Pero al volverse consciente de Krsna, la mujer se libera en esta vida y en la siguiente; de modo que la conciencia de Krsna es el verdadero movimiento de liberación de la mujer.

En el movimiento para la conciencia de Krsna, ¿cuál es la posición de las devotas?

Desde el punto de vista espiritual, hombres y mujeres (y todas las demás entidades vivientes) son iguales. El alma dentro del cuerpo es parte integral de Krsna y es igual en calidad y cantidad a todas las demás almas. Pero desde el punto de vista material, corporal, obviamente hay diferencias.

¿Qué pasa con esas diferencias?

En lo que se refiere a servir a Krsna y predicar el proceso de conciencia de Krsna, se ignoran las diferencias materiales. Hombres y mujeres sirven a la forma de la Deidad del Señor Supremo en el templo. adorando, limpiando, cocinando, y salen todos los días a predicar a las personas interesadas y distribuir literatura sobre la conciencia de Krsna. Las mujeres con hijos cuidan a sus hijos y difunden la conciencia de Krsna junto con sus hermanas espirituales solteras. En realidad, criar a un niño como devoto de Dios es en sí mismo un gran servicio a Krsna.

Pero he oído que pensáis que las mujeres son inferiores a los hombres. ¿Es eso cierto?

Como mencioné antes, espiritualmente no hay distinción. En el Bhagavad Gita, Krsna dice que tanto los hombres como las mujeres son elegibles para alcanzar el destino más elevado, regresar al reino de Dios, si se refugian en Él. Espiritualmente, no existe dualidad, ni inferioridad o superioridad basada en el cuerpo y la mente. Pero existen diferencias materiales: las mujeres generalmente no son tan fuertes físicamente como los hombres, pero solo las mujeres pueden tener hijos y nutrirlos con su leche materna. Por lo tanto, es natural que las ocupaciones de las mujeres sean diferentes a las de los hombres en algunos aspectos.

Hoy mucha gente rechaza esa idea. No hará que su movimiento sea muy popular entre las mujeres.

No podemos cambiar la filosofía de la conciencia de Krsna para hacerla popular. Ni estamos tan preocupados por la popularidad. Simplemente queremos presentar las enseñanzas de Krsna tal como son, y los que escuchen con receptividad se beneficiarán. El primer paso en la comprensión espiritual es trascender el concepto corporal de la vida. Pensar «soy un hombre» o «soy una mujer» es estar en ilusión. El cuerpo es un vestido material para el alma, un vestido que cambia de un momento a otro, a medida que el cuerpo se desarrolla, y cambiará por completo al morir. Pero la constante dentro del cuerpo es el alma, que no está sujeta al nacimiento, la muerte ni ninguna otra influencia material.

¿Cómo se relacionan los hombres y las mujeres en el proceso de conciencia de Krsna?

Excepto su esposa, un hombre consciente de Krsna debe considerar a toda mujer como lo haría con su madre. Entonces, en el proceso de conciencia de Krsna, la relación entre hombres y mujeres no se basa en la lujuria sino en el respeto mutuo, con Krsna en el centro. Si esta mentalidad está ausente, hombres y mujeres se mezclan con demasiada libertad, su relación puede fácilmente degradarse a la inmoralidad y la explotación mutua.

¿Qué pasa con las relaciones más íntimas?

En las relaciones más íntimas, el hombre protege a la mujer. En la juventud de una mujer, su padre la protege; después en el matrimonio, su esposo la protege; y en la vida posterior tiene a sus hijos mayores para protegerla.

¿Qué quieres decir con «proteger»?

La protección contra la ilusión y la degradación es la protección real. Cuando el padre, esposo e hijos están completamente entrenados en los principios espirituales por el maestro espiritual, ellos están capacitados para guiar a los miembros de su familia. El Bhagavad-Gita señala que cuando la tradición religiosa en la familia se derrumba, la castidad y la fidelidad de las mujeres se tambalean. Cuando hombres y mujeres se mezclan libremente, el resultado es el aborto y la progenie no deseada. De tal inmoralidad y libertinaje provienen los hogares rotos y la degradación de toda la sociedad. Por el contrario, cuando el esposo es un devoto dedicado de Dios y la esposa es leal, de ello resulta una buena progenie y una sociedad armoniosa.

¿Pero qué pasa si la mujer quiere ser más que una simple ama de casa?

Eso es bienvenido y animado. Las mujeres del movimiento para la conciencia de Krsna cantan, escriben, fotografían, pintan, actúan, esculpen, enseñan y bailan. Una de mis hermanas espirituales en nuestra comunidad agrícola de West Virginia es arquitecta. Ahora está ayudando a planificar un enorme templo que los devotos construirán durante los próximos años. De modo que no hay ningún obstáculo para una mujer que quiera usar sus talentos especiales para servir a Krsna.

¿Pueden las mujeres convertirse en líderes?

El verdadero liderazgo es enseñar con el ejemplo y el precepto de cómo volver a casa, volver a Dios, al final de esta vida. Un devoto plenamente consciente de Krsna, ya sea hombre o mujer, es en realidad más líder que todos los supuestos líderes de la sociedad moderna. Los líderes materialistas simplemente aumentan nuestros problemas materiales. Un devoto reduce esos problemas a cero. Eso es liderazgo real.

 

Los cuatro Yugas

Un Yuga es una época con características específicas; en cada Yuga aparecen diferentes encarnaciones de Krsna. Cuatro Yugas forman un ciclo, mil de los cuales forman un período de luz diurna en la vida del Señor Brahma; esa es también la duración de una noche en su vida.

Un 

Satya-yuga

 es la Edad de Oro que dura 1.600.000 años, en la que el proceso de la autorrealización es la meditación en Vishnu. En este momento, el noventa y nueve por ciento de la población se encuentra en la plataforma más elevada de existencia espiritual y el promedio de vida es de cien mil años.

Treta-yuga

 es la Edad de Plata que dura 1.200.000 años, en la que el proceso de autorrealización son sacrificios opulentos. La conciencia de Dios se reduce al setenta y cinco por ciento de la población y el promedio de vida es de diez mil años.

Dvapara-yuga

 es la Edad del Bronce que dura 800.000 años, en la que el proceso de autorrealización es una elaborada adoración a la Deidad en los templos. La conciencia de Dios se reduce al cincuenta por ciento de la población y el promedio de vida es de mil años.

Kali-yuga

 es la Edad de Hierro de disputas e hipocresía que dura 432.000 años, en la cual el proceso de autorrealización es sankirtana, el canto de los santos nombres de Dios. La conciencia de Dios se reduce al veinticinco por ciento de la población y disminuye constantemente, y la duración máxima de la vida es de cien años y también está disminuyendo. Ya hemos pasado 5.000 años del actual Kali-yuga. Al final, si una persona llega a los veinticinco años se considerará anciano y la única comida será la carne. No habrá productos agrícolas, cereales ni productos lácteos.

Después de la destrucción que sigue al final de Kali-yuga, Satya-yuga comienza de nuevo. Por tanto, el ciclo de Yugas es continuo como el ciclo de las estaciones.

 

Los ríos santos

El Ganges

El Ganges es sagrado porque es agua que emana del dedo del pie del Señor Vishnu. Cualquiera que se bañe en el Ganges se libera de reacciones pecaminosas. Se observa que el Ganges nunca se vuelve impuro o contaminado, incluso cuando se encuentran sustancias sucias en sus aguas. Muchas personas enfermas se curan después de bañarse y beber regularmente el agua del Ganges. Cuando un devoto deja su cuerpo, los restos se incineran y las cenizas se arrojan al Ganges.

El Señor Vamanadeva le pidió tres pasos de tierra a Bali Maharaja. Con el primer paso abarcó el planeta. Su segundo paso abarcó el universo y Su dedo del pie traspasó la cubierta del universo de modo que las aguas trascendentales del Océano Causal se filtraron. Estas son las aguas del Ganges. También se dice que el Ganges comenzó cuando el Señor Brahma lavó los pies de loto del Señor Vamanadeva con el agua de su kamandalu. En otra parte se dice que el Ganges vino a la tierra por pedido de Bhagiratha Maharaja.

El Ganges llegó primero a los planetas celestiales, pero tenía miedo de venir a la tierra porque ella caería con tanta fuerza que continuaría viajando a los planetas infernales donde mucha gente pecadora se bañaría en sus aguas y así depositaría sus pecados. Entonces se dispuso que el Señor Shiva lo impidiera atrapándose el agua en la cabeza. A la Madre Ganges también se le informó que las personas santas se bañarían en sus aguas, lo que contrarrestaría los efectos de las personas pecadoras.

El Yamuna

En el momento de la aparición del Señor Krsna, Su padre Vasudeva cruzó el Yamuna unos diez kilómetros río abajo (desde Vrndavana) a Mathura llevando al Bebé Señor en sus brazos. Aunque las aguas del río se habían separado dulcemente, el Niño Krsna se cayó del firme agarre de su padre debido a una fuerte ola. La diosa Yamuna se le apareció a Vasudeva acariciando suavemente a Krsna y le explicó que lo había arrebatado a propósito para tener la oportunidad de acariciarlo con afecto.

Debido a este incidente, y debido a que el Señor solía realizar Sus pasatiempos en sus orillas arenosas o dentro de sus aguas, los devotos de Krsna consideran que Yamuna es el río más sagrado de la India. Nacida como la hija del dios sol, la diosa Yamuna en forma de río nace en el Himalaya y fluye por mil doscientos kilómetros hasta que se encuentra con el Ganges en Prayag (Allahabad).

Los peregrinos se deleitan al bañarse en sus aguas durante todo el año porque saben que al hacerlo, los pecados de millones de vidas pasadas se borran. Sin embargo, durante el mes de Karttika (octubre-noviembre), el efecto purificador espiritual de bañarse en sus aguas se magnifica mil veces.
lessons.content_en:
VEDIC CULTURE

Definition of Hinduism

When we speak of Krsna consciousness, we must not confuse it with Hinduism. Krsna consciousness originates from the ancient Vedic Scriptures; Hinduism is not mentioned at all in the Vedas. Furthermore, the current popular usage of the term "Hinduism" does not correspond to its original meaning.

The word "Hindu" is a mispronunciation from Persian of "Sindhu," the Sanskrit name for the Indus River. For the Mughal invaders, who entered India by crossing the Indus, it was natural to refer to the conquered territory as the Land of the Indus River, or Hindustan. Thus, a "Hindu" is, properly speaking, an inhabitant of Hindustan, regardless of their religion.

The British, however, used "Hindu" to refer to Indians who were not Muslims, Buddhists, Sikhs, Jains, or members of other religious groups for which the British had names. "Hindu" was used as an umbrella term to literally describe thousands of quite different religious and cultural patterns in the vast area of the Indian subcontinent. Therefore, speaking of "Hinduism" is so misleading that it is meaningless. Our practice, in accordance with the teachings of the Vedic scriptures, is properly called sanatana-dharma, which translates best as "eternal law." However, for ordinary usage, Srila Prabhupada has taught us that the clearest and most informative name for our practice is Krsna consciousness.

Varnasrama-dharma, the Vedic social system

Varnasrama-dharma is the Vedic social system that divides society into two sets of four divisions, the first set based on occupation and the second based on spiritual position (asrama).

The four varnas (occupations) are the brahmanas, who are the intellectuals; the ksatriyas, warriors and administrators; the vaisyas, farmers and merchants; and the sudras, wage laborers. A person's place within this system is determined by their qualities and activities, not by their birth, and they must receive education to qualify for a particular position. The duties of the four varnas can be briefly summarized as follows:

The 

brahmanas

 are teachers who instruct both by example and precept. They must know the moral principles and be self-controlled. They also serve by guiding the ksatriyas to ensure that all people follow religious principles.

The 

ksatriyas

 administer according to the instructions received from the brahmanas and the Scriptures. They must be completely religious. They are known as "naradeva" or representatives of God on earth. If there is a battle, they personally lead the military forces.

The 

vaisyas

 are in charge of agriculture, trade, and cow protection. Since the purpose of society is to conduct a simple spiritual life, there is no production or trade of goods for an excessively luxurious life or sinful activities.

The 

sudras

 are the working class and serve by assisting the other three classes.

The social body is compared to the human body, with the brahmanas as the head, the ksatriyas as the arms, the vaisyas as the stomach, and the sudras as the legs. This is a natural system for dividing labor, not an artificial caste system. Within this system, everyone gradually rises to spiritual realization under the guidance of the brahmanas, in a social system that meets the real needs of all its members without disturbing their spiritual progress.

The four asramas represent stages within an individual's life as they grow in spiritual consciousness and renunciation. They are brahmacarya or student life; grhastha or married life; vanaprastha, or retired life; and sannyasa, or the order of renounced life.

One becomes a brahmacari at the age of five and lives with the spiritual master until the age of twenty-five. They receive both spiritual and material education, preparing them to understand the need to develop spiritual consciousness throughout their life. Simultaneously, they are trained in their varna or occupation.

At the age of twenty-five, the brahmacari may leave the gurukula and take a wife. Their training will qualify them to be a capable husband who can guide their family toward spiritual realization. At fifty, they retire from family life and visit holy places with their wife; this is vanaprastha. At the age of seventy-five, they entrust the care of their wife to their eldest son and adopt sannyasa, fully preparing to return to God by constantly chanting the Lord's name and serving Him without any other occupation.

Questions and Answers about Women in Krsna Consciousness

(by Visakha-devi dasi)

Why should women be interested in Krsna consciousness?

To realize that they are not women! By identifying ourselves as "women," we reveal the ignorance of our true identity. The word "woman" refers to the temporary material body, not the eternal spiritual soul, the person within the body. In perhaps forty or sixty years, the body I currently possess will perish, and I will no longer be a woman. But the living force, the soul within the body, does not perish. Krsna consciousness equips us with the understanding of the relationship of the soul with matter and with the Supreme Soul, Krsna. Human intelligence serves to meditate on these topics, not to dwell in materialistic thoughts based on false identification with the temporary body.

What do you think about the women's liberation movement?

The highest liberation is to return home, back to God, and never again face the miseries of birth, death, old age, disease, and death. While the women's liberation movement may offer some valuable proposals, it generally overlooks this supreme goal. However, by becoming Krsna conscious, a woman is liberated in this life and the next; thus, Krsna consciousness is the true women's liberation movement.

In the Krsna consciousness movement, what is the position of the female devotees?

From a spiritual perspective, men and women (and all other living entities) are equal. The soul within the body is an integral part of Krsna and is equal in quality and quantity to all other souls. But from a material, bodily perspective, there are obviously differences.

What about those differences?

In terms of serving Krsna and preaching the process of Krsna consciousness, the material differences are disregarded. Men and women serve the Deity form of the Supreme Lord in the temple, worshiping, cleaning, cooking, and going out daily to preach to interested people and distribute literature on Krsna consciousness. Women with children care for their children and spread Krsna consciousness alongside their single spiritual sisters. In fact, raising a child as a devotee of God is itself a great service to Krsna.

But I have heard that you think women are inferior to men. Is that true?

As I mentioned earlier, spiritually there is no distinction. In the Bhagavad-gita, Krsna says that both men and women are eligible to attain the highest destination, returning to the kingdom of God, if they take shelter of Him. Spiritually, there is no duality, nor inferiority or superiority based on body and mind. But there are material differences: women are generally not as physically strong as men, but only women can bear children and nourish them with their breast milk. Therefore, it is natural that women's occupations differ from men's in some respects.

Today, many people reject that idea. It will not make your movement very popular among women.

We cannot change the philosophy of Krsna consciousness to make it popular. Nor are we particularly concerned with popularity. We simply want to present the teachings of Krsna as they are, and those who listen with receptivity will benefit. The first step in spiritual understanding is to transcend the bodily concept of life. Thinking "I am a man" or "I am a woman" is to be in illusion. The body is a material dress for the soul, a dress that changes moment to moment as the body develops, and it will completely change upon death. But the constant within the body is the soul, which is not subject to birth, death, or any other material influence.

How do men and women relate in the process of Krsna consciousness?

Except for his wife, a Krsna conscious man should consider every woman as he would his mother. Thus, in the process of Krsna consciousness, the relationship between men and women is not based on lust but on mutual respect, with Krsna at the center. If this mentality is absent, men and women mix too freely, and their relationship can easily degrade into immorality and mutual exploitation.

What about more intimate relationships?

In more intimate relationships, the man protects the woman. In a woman's youth, her father protects her; later in marriage, her husband protects her; and in later life, she has her grown children to protect her.

What do you mean by "protect"?

Protection from illusion and degradation is real protection. When the father, husband, and children are fully trained in spiritual principles by the spiritual master, they are qualified to guide the members of their family. The Bhagavad-gita points out that when religious tradition in the family collapses, the chastity and fidelity of women waver. When men and women mix freely, the result is abortion and unwanted progeny. From such immorality and debauchery come broken homes and the degradation of society as a whole. Conversely, when the husband is a dedicated devotee of God and the wife is loyal, good progeny and a harmonious society result.

But what if a woman wants to be more than just a housewife?

That is welcomed and encouraged. Women in the Krsna consciousness movement sing, write, photograph, paint, act, sculpt, teach, and dance. One of my spiritual sisters in our farming community in West Virginia is an architect. She is currently helping to plan a huge temple that the devotees will build over the coming years. Therefore, there is no obstacle for a woman who wishes to use her special talents to serve Krsna.

Can women become leaders?

True leadership is teaching by example and precept how to return home, back to God, at the end of this life. A fully Krsna conscious devotee, whether male or female, is actually more of a leader than all the so-called leaders of modern society. Materialistic leaders simply increase our material problems. A devotee reduces those problems to zero. That is real leadership.

The Four Yugas

A Yuga is an era with specific characteristics; in each Yuga, different incarnations of Krsna appear. Four Yugas form a cycle, a thousand of which make up a daytime period in the life of Lord Brahma; that is also the duration of one night in his life.

Satya-yuga is the Golden Age that lasts 1,600,000 years, during which the process of self-realization is meditation on Vishnu. At this time, ninety-nine percent of the population is on the highest platform of spiritual existence, and the average lifespan is one hundred thousand years.

Treta-yuga is the Silver Age that lasts 1,200,000 years, during which the process of self-realization involves opulent sacrifices. God consciousness is reduced to seventy-five percent of the population, and the average lifespan is ten thousand years.

Dvapara-yuga is the Bronze Age that lasts 800,000 years, during which the process of self-realization is elaborate deity worship in temples. God consciousness is reduced to fifty percent of the population, and the average lifespan is one thousand years.

Kali-yuga is the Iron Age of disputes and hypocrisy that lasts 432,000 years, during which the process of self-realization is sankirtana, the chanting of the holy names of God. God consciousness is reduced to twenty-five percent of the population and is constantly decreasing, with the maximum lifespan being one hundred years, which is also declining. We have already passed 5,000 years of the current Kali-yuga. In the end, if a person reaches twenty-five years of age, they will be considered old, and the only food will be meat. There will be no agricultural products, grains, or dairy products.

After the destruction that follows the end of Kali-yuga, Satya-yuga begins anew. Thus, the cycle of Yugas is continuous, like the cycle of the seasons.

The Holy Rivers

The Ganges

The Ganges is sacred because it is water that emanates from the toe of Lord Vishnu. Anyone who bathes in the Ganges is freed from sinful reactions. It is observed that the Ganges never becomes impure or contaminated, even when dirty substances are found in its waters. Many sick people are cured after regularly bathing in and drinking the water of the Ganges. When a devotee leaves their body, the remains are cremated, and the ashes are thrown into the Ganges.

Lord Vamanadeva asked Bali Maharaja for three steps of land. With the first step, He covered the planet. His second step encompassed the universe, and His toe pierced the covering of the universe so that the transcendental waters of the Causal Ocean filtered through. These are the waters of the Ganges. It is also said that the Ganges began when Lord Brahma washed the lotus feet of Lord Vamanadeva with the water from his kamandalu. In another account, it is said that the Ganges came to Earth at the request of Bhagiratha Maharaja.

The Ganges first reached the heavenly planets, but it was afraid to come to Earth because it would fall with such force that it would continue traveling to the hellish planets where many sinful people would bathe in its waters and thus deposit their sins. It was arranged that Lord Shiva would prevent this by catching the water in his hair. Mother Ganges was also informed that saintly people would bathe in her waters, which would counteract the effects of sinful individuals.

The Yamuna

At the time of Lord Krsna's appearance, His father Vasudeva crossed the Yamuna about ten kilometers downstream (from Vrndavana) to Mathura, carrying the Baby Lord in his arms. Although the waters of the river parted gently, Baby Krsna fell from his father's firm grip due to a strong wave. The goddess Yamuna appeared to Vasudeva, gently caressing Krsna, and explained that she had purposely taken him away to have the opportunity to affectionately embrace him.

Due to this incident, and because the Lord used to perform His pastimes on its sandy banks or within its waters, Krsna devotees consider the Yamuna to be the most sacred river in India. Born as the daughter of the sun god, the goddess Yamuna in the form of a river is born in the Himalayas and flows for one thousand two hundred kilometers until it meets the Ganges at Prayag (Allahabad).

Pilgrims delight in bathing in its waters throughout the year because they know that by doing so, the sins of millions of past lives are erased. However, during the month of Karttika (October-November), the purifying spiritual effect of bathing in its waters is magnified a thousand times.
